name: ABC Arizona
designer: Elias Hanzer with Alex Lescieux
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Arizona as the first ever sans-to-serif superfamily packaged into a single variable font file, with five looks (Serif, Text, Mix, Flare, Sans) and three widths (Standard, Condensed, Compressed) at five weights plus italics.
summary: A five-genre superfamily by Elias Hanzer that travels from high-contrast serif to humanist grotesque inside a single variable font file.
description: A sans-to-serif superfamily designed by Elias Hanzer with Alex Lescieux that bundles Serif, Text, Mix, Flare and Sans into one variable font. Each subfamily carries three widths (Standard, Condensed, Compressed) across five weights with italics, so a single license can move from Renaissance-flavoured display serif into a humanist grotesque without leaving the file. Reach for it when an editorial system or campaign needs to flex tone across the same letterforms.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Arizona as a flexible and elegant superfamily with fluctuating serifs, calling it the first ever sans-to-serif variable font. They frame one end of the axis as a high-contrast, pointy design that flows into a straightforward grotesque with a human touch, and split the design space across separate variable files because the full palette is unusually large.

name: ABC Asfalt
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb), Olga Umpeleva
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Asfalt's elongated, tightly-packed forms as drawn from painted road lettering, stretched to compensate for the low perspective of an approaching car. The starting point was a stencilled version of road lettering from Niggli's mid-century Lettera series plus a Brazilian road-signage manual.
summary: A wide-to-narrow display sans drawn from painted road lettering, with five widths from Compressed to Expanded across four weights.
description: A display sans by Fabian Harb and Olga Umpeleva that takes its forms from the elongated road-painted lettering you read from a driver's seat. Five widths run from Compressed through Normal to Expanded, each at Light, Regular, Medium and Bold, giving a full lockup system for posters, packaging and identity work. Pulls in vernacular Brazilian signage and a stencilled cut of Niggli's Lettera as source material.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo say Asfalt's elongated, tightly-packed forms are drawn from painted road letters, stretched to compensate for the low perspective of an approaching car so words like STOP and GO read perfectly compact. They cite a stencilled version of road lettering from Niggli's mid-century Lettera series and a Brazilian road-surface signage manual found in a Rio thrift store as the design's starting points.

name: ABC Bingo
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer), original by Rob Roy Kelly
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Bingo as Johannes Breyer's first hit, drawn in 2011 while he was an intern at NORM and based on a sample published in Rob Roy Kelly's American Wood Type 1829-1900, with inner and outer contours drawn with polygons so the shapes read smooth at small scale and brutal when large.
summary: A polygonal wood-type display drawn by Johannes Breyer from a Rob Roy Kelly sample, smooth at small sizes and brutal at large.
description: A display face by Johannes Breyer based on a sample from Rob Roy Kelly's American Wood Type: 1829-1900, redrawn so its inner and outer contours are made of polygons rather than smooth curves. The result reads softly at body size and reveals jagged edges at billboard scale. Comes in Regular and Condensed widths with a thin monolinear punctuation set added in 2012.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Bingo as rough on the outside and soft on the inside, with an edgy exterior contrasting sympathetic, rounded inner contours. They credit it as Johannes Breyer's first hit in 2011, released while he was an intern at NORM via a purpose-built website and based on a sample from Rob Roy Kelly's American Wood Type: 1829-1900.

name: ABC Bubblegum
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Michelangelo Nigra, Marc Rouault)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Bubblegum as soft and silly but also geometric and highly modular, with built-in small caps and a monospaced companion, distinguishing it from typical 'bubbly' free fonts.
summary: A geometric, modular bubble display by Fabian Harb with built-in small caps and a monospaced companion.
description: A bubble display by Fabian Harb with Michelangelo Nigra and Marc Rouault that fuses soft, rounded shapes with strict modular geometry. Small caps are baked into the same font, and a monospaced companion is included for tabular work. A counterpoint to the looser bubble fonts found on the free-font fringes.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Bubblegum as soft and silly but also geometric and highly modular, calling it a sticky clash of two extremes. They note that small caps have been baked inside the typeface and that a monospaced companion is available for anyone brave enough to use it for invoicing.

name: ABC Camera
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejía, Sascha Bente)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Camera as a reinterpretation of light traps used in 1960s and 1970s television to keep text readable through screen blur, scaled up as 'holes' and offered alongside a Plain version without them.
summary: A neutral grotesque that scales up 60s and 70s TV light traps as visible holes, with a Plain version and matching mono.
description: A grotesque by Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejia and Sascha Bente that turns the light traps once used to keep TV text readable into a feature, scaling them up into visible holes. A Plain cut without the holes is provided alongside, and both versions run across five weights with italics plus a monospaced sibling. Sits close to Helvetica in tone but with freer proportions and a narrower body.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Camera as a neutral, toned-down grotesque that reimagines light traps originally used in the 60s and 70s to keep text readable on TV screens. They explain that screen blur once filled the holes so each letter looked complete, and that the design was a long-requested early concept of co-founder Fabian Harb scaled up for the digital age.

name: ABC Camera Rounded
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejía, Sascha Bente)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Camera Rounded as the rounded sibling of ABC Camera, sharing the light-traps concept (scaled up as holes) with a softened terminal treatment, available as Camera Rounded, Camera Rounded Plain and matching Mono cuts.
summary: The rounded sibling of ABC Camera, taking the same TV light-trap concept into a softer terminal treatment with hole, plain and mono cuts.
description: A rounded grotesque by Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejia and Sascha Bente that takes the Camera concept, scaled-up TV light traps, into softer terminals. Hole and Plain versions sit alongside Mono cuts across five weights with italics. Useful where a tech-leaning grotesque needs to read warmer without losing its screen-era reference.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Camera as a neutral, toned-down grotesque that reimagines 1960s and 70s television light traps for the digital age, available in hole and plain versions across five weights with italics. Camera Rounded extends the same idea with rounded terminals and matching mono styles.

name: ABC Connect
designer: Fabian Harb, Louis Brousseaud, Frédéric Jaman, Johannes Breyer, Oliver-Selim Boualam, Lukas Marstaller
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Connect as a digital dot font based on the ABC C-o-n-n-e-c-t-o-r-s grid, available in monospaced and proportional styles with corresponding italics, plus five screw-head dot styles (Flat, Torx, Hex, Nail, Phillips).
summary: A digital dot font built on the ABC Connectors grid, with proportional and monospaced cuts, italics, and five screw-head dot styles.
description: A dot-matrix display by Fabian Harb with Louis Brousseaud, Frederic Jaman, Johannes Breyer, Oliver-Selim Boualam and Lukas Marstaller, built on the ABC Connectors physical grid system. Comes in monospaced and proportional styles, both with matching italics, and ships with five screw-head dot variants: Flat, Torx, Hex, Nail and Phillips. Dinamo describe it as probably the world's first italic dot font.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Connect as a digital dot font based on the ABC C-o-n-n-e-c-t-o-r-s grid, claiming it is probably the world's first italic dot font. They list monospaced and proportional styles with corresponding italics, and five common screw head styles including Flat, Torx, Hex, Nail and Phillips.

name: ABC Cowboy
designer: Dinamo
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Cowboy as a straightforward western-style slab serif with 643 glyphs, 11,217 kerning pairs and 397 accented characters covering more than 127 languages, featuring Dinamo butterfly serifs and zero alternate letterforms.
summary: A western-style slab serif with Dinamo butterfly serifs, 643 glyphs and 11,217 kerning pairs, drawn for headlines.
description: A western-style slab serif from Dinamo carrying the studio's signature butterfly serifs, drawn as a single Regular cut with no alternates. The character set runs to 643 glyphs balanced by 11,217 kerning pairs, with 397 accented characters covering more than 127 languages, plus subscripts, superscripts and arbitrary fractions. Built for headline and packaging work where a saloon-era slab usually arrives sloppily produced.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Cowboy as a straightforward western-style slab serif and frame the genre as generally overlooked and often poorly produced. They highlight 643 glyphs balanced by 11,217 kerning pairs, 397 accented characters covering over 127 languages, Dinamo-style butterfly serifs and zero alternate letterforms.

name: ABC Daily
designer: Fabian Harb, Michelangelo Nigra, Connor Davenport
classification_notes: The family splits into Scotch and Slab siblings sharing the same skeleton and proportions, placing it across two serif traditions; the Scotch branch aligns with high-contrast modulated serifs while the Slab branch uses squared, unbracketed serifs throughout.
summary: A serif family built around two sibling branches, Scotch and Slab, designed to substitute for one another in the same way a designer might switch between roman and italic.
description: ABC Daily pairs a high-contrast Scotch serif with a squared Slab counterpart, both drawn to identical proportions, cap heights and x-heights so they can be mixed freely within a layout. The contrast between the two can read as quiet harmony or deliberate tension depending on how they are combined. Each branch comes in six weights with italics, plus monospaced variants, and the family includes Latin Extended and Greek coverage alongside a variable font axis.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Daily as a family of two siblings, Slab and Scotch, sharing key DNA features including proportions, skeleton, cap height and x-height. The intention is that either subfamily can substitute for the other in the way a designer might use roman and italic to create hierarchy, with visual results ranging from loving harmony to a sense of total destruction. Design is by Fabian Harb with Michelangelo Nigra and Connor Davenport, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.

name: ABC Diatype
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti, Andree Paat)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Diatype as a warm yet sharp grotesque rooted in the Swiss Neo-grotesque tradition, with shapes informed by pre-digital diatype typesetting machines, available in five weights with italics plus monospaced and semi-monospaced styles and a wide multiscript range.
summary: A warm Swiss neo-grotesque named after pre-digital typesetting machines, built out across widths, mono cuts and ten scripts.
description: A Swiss neo-grotesque by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb with Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti and Andree Paat, named for the diatype typesetting machines whose mechanics inform its shapes. The system spans five widths from Compressed to Expanded, monospaced and semi-monospaced cuts, and ten scripts including Arabic, Devanagari, Thai, Hangul and Georgian. A workhorse for screen reading where Helvetica feels too cool.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Diatype as a warm yet sharp grotesque ideal for text and reading on screen, with the name referring to the clunky pre-digital typesetting machines that informed its shapes and the wider Swiss Neo-grotesque genre. They credit Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb leading the design with a wider Dinamo team plus Vietnamese contributions from Đức Cao.

name: ABC Diatype Extra
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti, Andree Paat)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Diatype Extra as the monospaced and semi-monospaced extension of Diatype, adding Compressed and Condensed widths to the Mono and Semi-Mono cuts so the Swiss neo-grotesque system covers tabular and code settings at narrow measures.
summary: A monospaced and semi-monospaced extension of Diatype in Compressed and Condensed widths for code, data and narrow UI.
description: An extension of Diatype by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb with Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti and Andree Paat that adds Compressed and Condensed widths to both the Mono and Semi-Mono cuts. The result fills out the Swiss neo-grotesque system for environments where vertical space is tight: code listings, tabular data, dashboard chrome and narrow UI columns.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Diatype as a warm yet sharp grotesque ideal for text and reading on screen, with shapes informed by pre-digital typesetting machines and the wider Swiss Neo-grotesque genre. Diatype Extra adds compressed and condensed widths to the monospaced and semi-monospaced cuts within that system.

name: ABC Diatype Rounded
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Elias Hanzer, Renan Rosatti & Arnaud Chemin)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Diatype Rounded as a warm, curvy sans informed by the Swiss Neo-grotesque genre and pre-digital typesetting machines, with rounded forms that read mechanically as if engraved with a chunky tool, available in proportional, mono and semi-mono cuts.
summary: The rounded sibling of Diatype, with proportional, mono and semi-mono cuts and a slightly mechanical, curvy feel.
description: A rounded neo-grotesque by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb with Elias Hanzer, Renan Rosatti and Arnaud Chemin, sharing Diatype's Swiss skeleton but softening every terminal. Comes in proportional, monospaced and semi-monospaced cuts across the wider Diatype weight range, with italics. Has a slightly engraved, mechanical feel rather than the squishy roundness of typical soft sans.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Diatype Rounded as a warm, curvy sans with friendly shapes informed by the Swiss Neo-grotesque genre and pre-digital typesetting machines. They call the overall vibe bold and a little pop, with an understated sense of humour, and note that the rounded forms feel oddly mechanical, as if each character was engraved with a chunky tool in one continuous line.

name: ABC Estragon
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Daniel Chessari)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Estragon as a redrawing of an ornamental headline wood type from Harrild & Sons' New & Abridged Specimens of Wood-letter (circa 1890), spotted on @typeTFA, with three combinable styles (Wildstyle, Arrow, Bubble) plus matching weights.
summary: A 19th-century ornamental wood-letter display redrawn in 2017, with three combinable styles (Wildstyle, Arrow, Bubble).
description: An ornamental wood-letter display by Fabian Harb and Daniel Chessari, redrawn from a circa-1890 Harrild & Sons specimen for contemporary use. Three distinctive styles, Wildstyle, Arrow and Bubble, can be freely mixed at the level of individual characters, alongside a base cut, across six weights from Thin to Heavy. Sam de Groot used it on the identity for Condo New York and described it as Schwitters-meets-Wildstyle.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo recount spotting Estragon's starting point on the Type Foundries Archive Twitter account @typeTFA, identified as a spread from Harrild & Sons' New & Abridged Specimens of Wood-letter circa 1890. They redrew the design in 2017 and added three distinctive styles, Wildstyle, Arrow and Bubble, that can be freely combined character by character.

name: ABC Favorit
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Erkin Karamemet, Immo Schneider, Arnaud Chemin, Ruggero Magrì)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Favorit as a straightforward, low-contrast grotesque combining geometric rigidity with subtle oddities and a humorous touch, with five weights plus italics, an underlined Lining cut, sub-families Extended, Expanded and Mono, and multiscript companions in Greek, Cyrillic, Hangul, Arabic, Hebrew, Devanagari, Georgian and Tifinagh.
summary: A low-contrast grotesque by Dinamo with geometric rigidity, subtle oddities, a smart-underline Lining cut and wide multiscript coverage.
description: A low-contrast grotesque by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb with Erkin Karamemet, Immo Schneider, Arnaud Chemin and Ruggero Magri, sitting between geometric rigidity and small humanist quirks. Five core weights with italics extend into Compressed, Condensed, Extended, Expanded and Mono cuts, plus Lining (with a built-in smart underline that connects descenders) and multiscript siblings across Greek, Cyrillic, Hebrew, Arabic, Devanagari, Georgian, Tifinagh and Hangul. Dinamo's identity workhorse.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Favorit as a straightforward, low-contrast grotesque that combines geometric rigidity with subtle oddities and a humorous touch, available in five weights with corresponding italics and including its own unique underline. They highlight Favorit Lining's smart underline that connects descending characters and conjoins initials and finals, and list ABC Favorit Greek, Cyrillic, Hangul and Arabic among the multiscript extensions.

name: ABC Gaisyr
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb) with Michelangelo Nigra
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Gaisyr as a typeface with Dinamo house-style Butterfly Serifs, taking its starting point from sketches by 18th-century royal typographer Jacques Jaugeon, with a tension between strict geometry and hand-held gestures, available in proportional, monospaced and semi-monospaced styles plus Greek and Cyrillic.
summary: A double-serif text family by Fabian Harb drawn from Jacques Jaugeon's 18th-century sketches for Louis XIV, with Mono and Semi-Mono cuts.
description: A serif by Fabian Harb with Michelangelo Nigra carrying Dinamo's house butterfly serifs, taking its point of departure from sketches by Jacques Jaugeon, the early 18th-century royal typographer for Louis XIV. Holds a tension between strict geometric rationality and looped, hand-held gestures, and runs across six weights plus italics in proportional, monospaced and semi-monospaced cuts, with Greek and Cyrillic extensions.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Gaisyr as a beautiful typeface with Dinamo house-style Butterfly Serifs, taking its point de depart from sketches by Jacques Jaugeon, the early 18th Century royal typographer for King Louis XIV. They note a tension between strict geometry and loopy hand-held gestures, and list proportional, monospaced and semi-monospaced styles.

name: ABC Galapagos
designer: Felix Salut with Dinamo (Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb)
classification_notes: A modular display typeface built from 42 cuts (rounded, straight, angular, plus rounded-straight, rounded-angular, straight-angular, and rounded-straight-angular hybrids), evolved from Felix Salut's Galapagos Game physical building blocks for drawing letters.
summary: A 42-cut modular display family grown from Felix Salut's Galapagos Game building blocks, with rounded, straight, angular and hybrid styles across five weights.
description: A modular display typeface by Felix Salut and Dinamo that descends from Salut's Galapagos Game, a physical set of building blocks designed for drawing letter shapes. The font is composed of 42 cuts spanning rounded, straight and angular styles with hybrids such as rounded-straight, rounded-angular, straight-angular, and rounded-straight-angular. Each style runs across five weights from Light to Black, with grid and no-grid versions, and stylistic alternates that move characters up and down a vertical grid. Reach for it on identity, exhibition and editorial work that wants a futuristic yet biological mood and a system you can recombine.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Galapagos as built from 42 different cuts of varying styles, with rounded, straight, and angular cuts plus rounded-straight, rounded-angular, straight-angular, and rounded-straight-angular hybrids. Each style comes in five weights with grid and no-grid versions, and stylistic alternates allow characters to be moved along a vertical grid. The typeface evolved from designer Felix Salut's Galapagos Game, a physical set of building blocks for drawing letter shapes.

name: ABC Ginto
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Dinamo describes ABC Ginto as an exuberant geometric-humanist typeface that plays the tension between circular and rectangular forms, remixing strict twentieth-century Modernist purity with the more baroque, animated phototypesetting styles of the 1950s and 1960s.
summary: A geometric-humanist sans by Seb McLauchlan that plays circular against rectangular, drawn from twentieth-century sans research and 1950s and 1960s phototype.
description: A geometric-humanist sans by London-based Seb McLauchlan, developed during research into twentieth-century sans-serif typefaces and the shift from Modernist purity to the more baroque, animated phototypesetting styles of the 1950s and 1960s. The system has two sister families. Ginto is compact and poised, with weights from Thin to Ultra, while Ginto Nord raises x-height and widens characters for chunky display use, with weights from Hairline to Ultra. Both come in standard and Condensed widths, and Ginto's identity work for Discord and others has made it a staple of contemporary brand systems.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo classify ABC Ginto as an exuberant geometric-humanist typeface that delights in tension, especially the tension between circular and rectangular forms. Designed by Seb McLauchlan, it remixes Modernist purity with the more baroque, animated styles of the 1950s and 1960s phototypesetting period. The system splits into Ginto, compact and poised, and Ginto Nord, which raises x-height and widens characters and stretches from Hairline to Ultra Bold.

name: ABC Ginto Rounded
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Ginto Rounded as a geometric-humanist font of soft edges and playful forms, exploring tension between circular shapes and moments of sharpness, with proportion models that nod to Futura.
summary: The softer cousin of Ginto, a rounded geometric-humanist family in three widths from compact text to chunky Nord display.
description: A rounded geometric-humanist sans by Seb McLauchlan, sister to Ginto, Ginto Nord and Ginto Nord Condensed. Standard Ginto Rounded keeps a regular x-height and compact lowercase for smaller text sizes. Ginto Nord Rounded raises the x-height and widens characters for chunky display work, and Ginto Rounded Nord Condensed handles narrower moments. A variable font ties both Nords together so widths can be tuned across spatial contexts. Eight weights with italics across all three widths give brand systems room to range from text to billboard.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Ginto Rounded as a geometric-humanist font of soft edges and playful forms. Designed by Seb McLauchlan, it explores the tension between circular shapes and moments of sharpness, with field-recording-like nods to baroque, animated 1960s and 1970s styles set against stricter modern proportion models like Futura. Standard Ginto Rounded suits smaller text sizes, Ginto Nord Rounded suits chunky display, and a variable font bridges both Nords.

name: ABC Gramercy
designer: Robert Janes
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Gramercy as an elegant contemporary serif balancing painterly, whimsical forms with functionality and sturdiness, initially informed by F.H. Ernst Schneidler's Amalthea (1956), the italic companion to his Schneidler-Mediaeval (1938), with flared strokes in place of ball terminals.
summary: A 48-style contemporary serif by Robert Janes with three optical sizes, painterly flared strokes and uppercase swashes in every weight.
description: A contemporary serif by Robert Janes that balances slightly painterly, whimsical forms with sturdiness for editorial use. The system runs to 48 styles across three optical sizes, Standard, Fine and Display, each carrying eight weights from Thin to Super with matching italics and uppercase swashes covering every weight and subfamily. Where another serif might draw ball terminals, Gramercy uses flared strokes, an idea seeded by F.H. Ernst Schneidler's Amalthea (1956), the italic companion to his Schneidler-Mediaeval (1938). Variable axes let designers move from high to low contrast and from Thin to Super.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Gramercy as an elegant, contemporary serif that balances painterly, whimsical forms with functionality and sturdiness. Designed by Robert Janes, it spans 48 styles across three subfamilies (Standard, Fine and Display), each with uppercase swashes. Gramercy was initially informed by F.H. Ernst Schneidler's Amalthea (1956), the italic companion to his eponymous Schneidler-Mediaeval (1938).

name: ABC Gravity
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Robert Janes, Fabiola Mejia, Olga Umpeleva)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Gravity as a one-weight, twelve-width headline typeface stretched and compressed from heavy 1960s and 1970s grotesques, with stylistic alternates that let characters jump above the baseline or hang from the top.
summary: A one-weight, twelve-width headline grotesque that runs from XXXX Compressed to Wide, with playful baseline-jumping alternates.
description: A headline display by Dinamo that takes a single heavy grotesque skeleton from the 1960s and 1970s and stretches it across twelve widths from XXXX Compressed to Wide. All twelve styles can be used separately or packaged as one variable font, modulating seamlessly between extremes. Stylistic alternates let characters jump over the baseline or hang from the top, with square or round punctuation, hairline punctuation, and raised descenders. Built for posters, magazine covers and identity systems where you want loud headlines and tight title arrangements.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Gravity as a one weight, 12 width headline typeface, with many stylistic alternates that allow for playful settings and super-compact title arrangements. Its skeleton was inspired by heavy grotesque typefaces from the 1960s and 1970s, compressed and stretched out into extreme widths. All 12 styles can be used separately or packaged together as a single variable font, modulated seamlessly from XXXX Compressed to Wide.

name: ABC Grow
designer: Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb
classification_notes: A modular, all-caps layered display typeface built from six stackable outline layers that can be combined into 63 configurations, placing it firmly in the display category with no direct analogue in conventional subcategories.
summary: A modular layered display typeface by Dinamo, built from six combinable outlines that produce 63 distinct visual configurations.
description: Inspired by a 1970s phototypesetting typeface structured in onion-like layers, ABC Grow was developed by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb using custom software to isolate and recombine six basic outlines. The result is 63 purchasable combinations, all-caps, with fractions, roman numerals, arrows and ligatures included. Alternative narrow characters for O and a tighter M offer a less blocky register when needed.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Grow as originating from Fabian Harb's discovery of a 1970s phototypesetting typeface made in distinct layers, like the rings of an onion. Custom software was developed to separate those layers and allow six basic outlines to be grouped into 63 different combinations. The display font is entirely uppercase and includes fractions, roman numerals, arrows and ligatures, with alternative characters offering a more economical feel. Each combination can be purchased individually or as a complete set.

name: ABC Helveesti
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Robert Janes, Johannes Breyer, Fabiola Mejia) with Andree Paat
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Helveesti as a digitisation of a Soviet-era Helvetica clone found in a 1970s Estonian children's book, with light traps (spiked corners stemming outwards) originally designed to compensate for blurring during the phototypesetting era.
summary: A digitisation of a Soviet-era Helvetica clone with phototype light traps, drawn from a 1970s Estonian children's book.
description: A neo-grotesque revival by Dinamo with Andree Paat, digitised from a Soviet-era Helvetica clone Fabian Harb found in a vintage Estonian children's book during a 2014 type class in Tallinn. The original light traps, spiked corners stemming outwards like mutated tails, were designed to compensate for blurring during phototypesetting, and the Spikes subfamily preserves them. Five weights from Regular to Black ship with matching italics across both Helveesti and Helveesti Spikes. Use it where you want Helvetica's voice with a folkloric, slightly mutant accent.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Helveesti as a digitisation of what appeared to be a Soviet-era clone of Helvetica, found by Fabian Harb in a vintage Estonian children's book during a Dinamo type class in Tallinn in winter 2014. The typeface's light traps, spiked corners stemming outwards like mutated tails, were designed to help compensate for blurring during the phototypesetting era. The family ships in five weights with italics, plus a Helveesti Spikes subfamily.

name: ABC Honeymoon
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Arnaud Chemin)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Honeymoon as a Dinamo-style monospaced script typeface, with single-width uppercase alternates, extravagant swashes and other celebratory alternates.
summary: A monospaced script with single-width capitals, swash symbols and celebratory alternates, made for friends and lovers.
description: A monospaced script by Dinamo's Fabian Harb and Arnaud Chemin, with seven weights from Hairline to Bold plus a Honeymoon Mix subfamily. Single-width uppercase alternates keep capitals on a fixed grid, while extravagant swash symbols (hearts, daggers, swans, hands), alternate punctuation, double-width numbers and circled numbers give the font a celebratory mood. Sits well in invitations, packaging, music graphics and any project that wants script warmth without losing typographic discipline.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Honeymoon as a true Dinamo-style monospaced script typeface for all our friends and lovers, containing romantic features such as single-width uppercase alternates, extravagant swashes, and other celebratory alternates.

name: ABC Ikarus
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Erkin Karamemet, Johannes Breyer)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Ikarus as a humanist double-family rooted in wayfinding, with a sturdy monolinear text design alongside an articulate, high-contrast Flair and Contrast companion for headlines and display, optimised for distance reading and small sizes.
summary: A humanist wayfinding double-family with a monolinear text cut and high-contrast Flair and Contrast companions across three widths and eight weights.
description: A humanist double-family by Dinamo's Fabian Harb, Erkin Karamemet and Johannes Breyer, rooted in wayfinding. One side runs as a sturdy, monolinear text design, the other as an articulate, high-contrast Flair and Contrast companion for headlines. Open terminals and clearly distinctive letterforms are tuned for distance reading, challenging light and the smallest sizes. Three widths (Condensed, Normal, Extended) carry eight weights per subfamily with matching italics, and a variable font axis ties everything together for seamless movement between extremes.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Ikarus as a humanist double-family: a sturdy, monolinear text design on one side, an articulate, high-contrast companion for headlines and display on the other. Rooted in wayfinding, it features open terminals and clearly distinctive letterforms, optimised for readability at a distance, in challenging light conditions, and at the smallest type sizes. Three widths (Condensed, Normal, and Extended), eight weights per subfamily, and matching italics give it serious range.

name: ABC Laica
designer: Alessio D'Ellena
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Laica as the result of a forced collaboration between the broad nib pen and the pointed nib pen, with chiselled transitions by default that can be flattened to straight transitions via a stylistic set, available in proportional and monospaced subfamilies.
summary: A contemporary serif by Alessio D'Ellena with chiselled transitions and a matching monospaced cut, born of a forced marriage between broad and pointed nib pens.
description: A contemporary serif by Alessio D'Ellena, sketched as a student at the Royal Academy of Art in The Hague between airport armchairs and Roman trams, that frames itself as a playful retort to calligraphy in an era of cheap flights and remote work. Chiselled transitions create rhythm and optical balance for text, and a stylistic set switches them to straight transitions for a simpler tone. Both proportional Laica and Laica Mono ship in Light, Book, Regular, Medium and Bold weights with matching italics, sitting comfortably across editorial, branding and code-adjacent product work.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Laica as the result of a cruel methodology: a forced collaboration between the broad nib pen and the pointed nib pen. Designed by Alessio D'Ellena, it features chiselled transitions by default that generate visual rhythm and optical balance for text, with a stylistic set that activates straight transitions for a simpler tone. Both monospaced and proportional sub-families come in Light, Book, Regular, Medium and Bold weights with corresponding italics.

name: ABC Marfa
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Seb McLauchlan)
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Marfa as stylistically born in the hot metal typesetting period, an homage to early American gothic typefaces, originally drawn as Gothic No. 6 by Fabian Harb and later extended by Seb McLauchlan into 28 styles with corresponding italics and a mono counterpart.
summary: A 28-style American gothic by Dinamo and Seb McLauchlan, with proportional, Semi-Mono and Mono cuts plus small caps and old style figures.
description: An American gothic sans by Fabian Harb and Seb McLauchlan, born in hot metal typesetting and originally drawn as Gothic No. 6 in homage to early American gothic typefaces. Early adopters included Other Means, Dan Solbach, Folder Studio, Eloise Harris and Nea-Kosma, and NODE. The current family runs to 28 styles across proportional Marfa, Marfa Semi-Mono and Marfa Mono, with seven weights and matching italics, plus small caps and old style figures as a nod to late nineteenth-century printing. Sits well in editorial, identity and product work that wants a sturdy, utilitarian voice with an American accent.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Marfa as stylistically born in the hot metal typesetting period, originally a sturdy, utilitarian typeface of three modest weights known as Gothic No. 6, drawn by Fabian Harb in homage to early American gothic typefaces. Designer Seb McLauchlan helped extend the font into a full family with corresponding italics and a Mono counterpart, totalling 28 styles, including small caps and old style figures as a nod to late 19th Century printing.

name: ABC Marist
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Marist as a warm, reader-friendly font with prominent serifs and wide uppercase proportions, inspired by the Old Style genre, specifically Nicolas Jenson's 15th Century Venetian roman and William Morris's 19th Century Golden Type.
summary: A warm Old Style serif by Seb McLauchlan, picking up threads from Jenson's roman and William Morris's Golden Type for contemporary reading.
description: An Old Style serif by Seb McLauchlan, drawn from afternoons in archives investigating Nicolas Jenson's highly legible 15th Century Venetian roman and William Morris's 19th Century Golden Type, his craft-focused reinterpretation of Jenson. Marist fine-tunes those threads for contemporary eyes and digital libraries, with prominent serifs and wide uppercase proportions that take their time. Six weights from Book to Black ship with matching italics and small caps, sitting comfortably in long-form editorial, books and brand systems that want warmth and historical depth.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Marist as a warm, reader-friendly font with prominent serifs and wide uppercase proportions that take their time. Designed by Seb McLauchlan, its forms are inspired by the Old Style genre, specifically Nicolas Jenson's 15th Century Venetian roman and William Morris's 19th Century Golden Type. Marist picks up some of Morris and Jenson's threads, fine-tuning their forms for contemporary eyes and digital libraries.

name: ABC Maxi
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Andree Paat
classification_notes: Rooted in mid-century Swiss International Style geometry with a warm, engineered character; the sharp and round variants, alongside monospaced subfamilies, place it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with an expressive, variable-driven edge.
summary: A variable type system in four subfamilies — Round, Round Mono, Sharp and Sharp Mono — drawing on mid-century Swiss geometry with angular lines and spaghetti-like movement.
description: Designed at Dinamo by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb with Andree Paat, ABC Maxi references the underlying skeleton of Josef Müller-Brockmann's 1958 CWS wordmark and the 1981 Swatch logo, reinterpreting Swiss International Style forms through a variable axis that runs from Hairline to Black. Rather than a conventional italic, the family offers two stylistic registers — Round and Sharp — each available in proportional and monospaced versions, giving users four distinct subfamilies. The variable font technology allows subtle shape manipulation that shifts the overall tone, making it equally suited to branding, editorial and screen work.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Maxi as a heavily-engineered yet warm and witty type system of spaghetti movements and angular lines, with familiar shapes informed by the long history of the International Style. Its forms — referencing mid-century and post-modern Swiss designs including Müller-Brockmann's CWS wordmark and the Swatch logo — can be stretched, altered and animated via variable font technology from Hairline to Black. Four subfamilies (Round, Round Mono, Sharp, Sharp Mono) stand in place of a standard italic, each offering subtle tonal differences through manipulations in shape.

name: ABC Monument Grotesk
designer: Kasper-Florio (Larissa Kasper, Rosario Florio) and Dinamo
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Monument Grotesk as a digitisation and reinterpretation of contours found in an online scan of Palmer & Rey's 1884 New Specimen Book, with a compact skeleton, high vertical contrast and surprisingly sharp end strokes, with honest, unrefined and idiosyncratic shapes.
summary: A grotesque by Kasper-Florio with Dinamo, drawn from contours in an 1884 Palmer & Rey specimen, with proportional, Semi-Mono and Mono cuts.
description: A grotesque by Swiss studio Kasper-Florio (Larissa Kasper and Rosario Florio) with Dinamo, traced back to contours found in an online scan of Palmer & Rey's 1884 New Specimen Book. Kasper-Florio digitised and reinterpreted the typeface as their house font, and it has been available via Dinamo since 2018. The current family covers eight weights with italics across proportional Monument Grotesk, Monument Grotesk Mono and Monument Grotesk Semi Mono, with a compact skeleton, high vertical contrast and sharp end strokes that read as honest and pleasingly unpolished.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Monument Grotesk as owing its starting point to contours that the Swiss design studio Kasper-Florio stumbled across in an online scan of Palmer & Rey's 1884 New Specimen Book. Kasper-Florio digitised and reinterpreted the typeface for contemporary use, and it has served as the studio's house typeface, available to the public via Dinamo since 2018. It features honest, unrefined, and idiosyncratic shapes, available in seven weights including Italic, Mono and Semi-Mono cuts, plus Heavy and Black with corresponding Italics.

name: ABC Oracle
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer) with Andree Paat, Thy Ha, Chi-Long Trieu, Daria Cohen
classification_notes: Dinamo describes Oracle as two families that began in the same place but wandered in different directions: an elegant sans serif with friendly, smooth contours and subtle contrast, and Oracle Triple, a strict-rhythm exploration that segments the bounding box into thirds with characters taking different numbers of units.
summary: A two-family humanist sans by Johannes Breyer: an elegant text sans plus Oracle Triple, a third-unit grid experiment in monospaced rhythm.
description: A two-family humanist sans by Dinamo's Johannes Breyer with Andree Paat, Thy Ha, Chi-Long Trieu and Daria Cohen. Standard Oracle is an elegant sans serif with friendly, smooth contours and subtle contrast, available in nine weights with italics and a deep set of stylistic alternates. Oracle Triple takes the logic of monospaced letterforms to an extreme, segmenting the bounding box into thirds with different characters taking different numbers of units. Sits well across identity, exhibition and editorial work that wants a calm humanist sans plus a more eccentric monospaced companion.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Oracle as two families that began in the same place but wandered off in entirely different directions. Oracle's first family is an elegant sans serif with friendly, smooth contours and subtle contrast, available in six weights plus italics, with a range of alternates and stylistic sets. Oracle Triple is an exploration of strict rhythm with silly results, taking the logic of monospaced letterforms to an extreme by segmenting its bounding box into units of thirds, with different characters taking up differing numbers of units.

name: ABC Otto
designer: Sam de Groot, Laura Opsomer Mironov
classification_notes: Rooted in the Dutch Baroque tradition of Miklós Kis, Otto descends from 17th-century 'le goût hollandois' serif forms, though with an enlarged x-height, rounder evenness and ahistorical proportions that distinguish it from strict revival work.
summary: A cheerful text serif by Sam de Groot and Laura Opsomer Mironov, loosely descended from the Baroque types of Miklós Kis but reshaped into something distinctly its own.
description: Otto began as an irreverent remix of 17th-century Baroque type by Amsterdam-based Hungarian punchcutter Miklós Kis, one of the central figures of 'le goût hollandois'. Where Kis is stern and jagged, Otto is round and wide, with a larger x-height and occasionally top-heavy proportions that make no claim to historical fidelity. The italics depart entirely from Baroque convention, replacing floral ornament with a bubbly, open simplicity and a strongly varying slope that lends the family its lilting rhythm.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Otto as a cheerful text typeface in four considered weights, designed by Amsterdam-based graphic designer Sam de Groot and type-designer-turned-actor Laura Opsomer Mironov. They position it as a typeface for all things worth reading, noting its sturdy wide rhythm and beautifully lilting italics, while acknowledging its origins as an irreverent remix of Miklós Kis that has since metamorphosed into a proudly ahistorical design.

name: ABC Paloma
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Sascha Bente, Jovana Jocic)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Paloma as translating triangle, circle and square into legible letterforms, deriving from avant-garde constructivist alphabets by Herbert Bayer (Universal Type, 1925), Jan Tschichold (Single Alphabet, 1929) and Max Bill (Konkrete Kunst, Kunsthalle Basel, 1949).
summary: A constructivist geometric sans drawn from triangle, circle and square, extending Bayer, Tschichold and Max Bill's avant-garde alphabets across seven weights.
description: A geometric sans by Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Sascha Bente, Jovana Jocic) that translates the most basic shapes of triangle, circle and square into legible letterforms. First drawn in 2021 for the visual identity of Bikini Books, it now spans seven weights from Thin to Ultra. Dinamo cite Herbert Bayer's Universal Type (1925), Jan Tschichold's Single Alphabet (1929) and Max Bill's Konkrete Kunst (Kunsthalle Basel, 1949) as references. Optically optimised with 33,753 kerning pairs and supporting 97 Latin-based languages, it suits identity work that wants modernist rigour without slipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Paloma as a typeface that translates triangle, circle and square into legible letterforms, organising a visually unique and systematically consistent archetype family. They cite Herbert Bayer (Universal Type, 1925), Jan Tschichold (Single Alphabet, 1929) and Max Bill (Konkrete Kunst, Kunsthalle Basel, 1949) as references. First drawn in 2021 for Bikini Books, it has been extended into a multi-weight design from Hairline to Ultra, covering 97 Latin-based languages and optically optimised with 33,753 kerning pairs. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Roberto Arista, Hugo Jourdan and Marte Verhaegen.

name: ABC Pareto
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Erkin Karamemet)
classification_notes: Dinamo position Pareto in the lineage of slab serifs that once appeared on the swing doors of American frontier saloons, but more Bauhaus 'Western' than Spaghetti, with a trilogy of circular, triangular and rectangular serif endings.
summary: A modular chunky slab with three swappable serif endings (triangular, circular, rectangular), reading more Bauhaus Western than Spaghetti.
description: A modular slab by Dinamo (Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb, with Erkin Karamemet) drawn from the long lineage of slab serifs once found on American frontier saloon swing doors, but rendered more Bauhaus Western than Spaghetti. Three individual font files cover circular, triangular and rectangular serif endings that can be mixed and matched, and the trio can be bought bundled. Latin and Cyrillic are supported. Reach for it on display work that wants chunky, flexible and slightly humorous slabs with modular swap-in detail.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Pareto as informed by a long lineage of slab serif typefaces that once appeared on the swing doors of American frontier saloons, more Bauhaus 'Western' than Spaghetti, featuring a trilogy of circular, triangular and rectangular serif endings to be mixed and matched. Designed by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb with Erkin Karamemet, spaced and kerned by Igino Marini, produced by Gustavo Ferreira. Three individual font files are available, plus a bundle of all three for flexible, humorous modularity, with Latin and Cyrillic support.

name: ABC Pelikan
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb with Fabiola Mejia)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Pelikan as an unpolished sans with a unicase feature baked inside that lets all letters extend to the same height. They tie it to the 1920s modernist 'fight against capital letters' and the 1970s search for an 'egalitarian' type treatment, and the unusually large x-height gives it a confident stance.
summary: An unpolished, large x-height sans with a same-height unicase feature, spanning standard, condensed, extended, semi-mono and mono cuts.
description: A grotesque sans by Dinamo (Fabian Harb with Fabiola Mejia) built around a unicase feature that extends every letter, big or small, to the same height. Pelikan ties this idea to the 1920s modernist fight against capitals and the 1970s search for an egalitarian unicase. The system covers five widths (Pelikan, Condensed, Extended, Semi Mono, Mono), each with eight weights from Thin to Black plus matching italics. Two unicase stylistic sets are included: ss01 (uppercase-heavy and sturdy) and ss02 (jumbled and playful). Latin and Greek are supported, with Hangul, Japanese and Chinese pairings recommended.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Pelikan as an unpolished sans with a special unicase feature baked inside, which lets you extend all letters, big or small, to the same height. They reference modernists in the 1920s waging a 'fight against capital letters' and the 1970s search for an 'egalitarian' type treatment via the unicase. The unusually large x-height gives it a confident stance and presence. Two unicase Stylistic Sets are offered: Unicase 1 is uppercase heavy and sturdy in text blocks, while Unicase 2 is jumbled and playful, mixing uppercase and lowercase forms at the same height. Designed by Fabian Harb with Fabiola Mejia, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Renan Rosatti.

name: ABC Prophet
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb, with Erkin Karamemet)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Prophet as inspired by Joseph Churchward's Georgina, combining warm calligraphic curves with a clean, machine-like sensibility, bridging the hand-drawn and mechanical genres. It is also the first typeface Dinamo co-founders Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb worked on together.
summary: A humanist sans inspired by Joseph Churchward's Georgina, marrying calligraphic warmth with mechanical clarity across six weights and italics.
description: A humanist sans by Dinamo (Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb, with Erkin Karamemet) inspired by Joseph Churchward's Georgina. Prophet bridges hand-drawn and mechanical genres, combining warm calligraphic curves with a clean machine-like sensibility. It carries family significance as the first typeface Dinamo's co-founders worked on together; first used on personal projects, then released in 2016 as a family of six weights with corresponding italics. Stylistic sets include an alternate single-storey 'a' (ss01) and tabular figures.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe the Prophet family as inspired by Joseph Churchward's Georgina, combining warm calligraphic curves with a clean, machine-like sensibility that bridges the hand-drawn and mechanical genres. They note it is somewhat prophetically the first typeface Dinamo co-founders Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb worked on together, used initially for their own personal projects, and after a production process spanning three countries, two timezones, two continents, four Skype accounts and one Dinamo world tour, the family of six weights with corresponding italics was released in 2016. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Chi-Long Trieu.

name: ABC Publisher
designer: Dinamo (Michelangelo Nigra, Fabian Harb, Marc Rouault)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Publisher as a typewriter-inspired design, re-upholstered for modern print and screen use; tracing the typewriter genre to Courier and ITC American Typewriter, with proportional spacing for long reading.
summary: A typewriter slab system with proportional spacing, paired with a Sans companion and a hybrid Mix, across normal and condensed widths in variable fonts.
description: A typewriter-inspired family by Dinamo (Michelangelo Nigra, Fabian Harb, Marc Rouault) re-upholstered for modern print and screen. Publisher embraces typewriter mechanical honesty and authorial voice while spacing letters proportionally for long reading. Three subfamilies share an underlying skeleton: a Slab (typewriter classic), a Sans companion, and a playful Mix that hybridises both, each in standard and Condensed widths across six weights with italics. Everything is delivered as variable fonts with fine-grained weight and width control. Engineering by Hugo Jourdan and Roberto Arista; campaign by Johannes Breyer and Melanie Schmidt.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Publisher as a typewriter-inspired design re-upholstered for modern print and screen use, embracing the genre's mechanical honesty and authorial voice while letters are proportionally spaced for long, untiring reading. They trace the typewriter genre to Courier and proportional interpretations like ITC American Typewriter. Three subfamilies (Slab, Sans, Mix) share the same underlying skeleton and design DNA so they can be mixed and matched. The family spans Condensed to Normal across six weights with corresponding italics, packaged as variable fonts. Designed by Michelangelo Nigra, Fabian Harb and Marc Rouault, with engineering by Hugo Jourdan and Roberto Arista.

name: ABC ROM
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Dinamo describe ROM as a sturdy, confident fusion of classic Grotesk and Gothic typeface styles, drawing on conceptual art catalogues from the 1960s and 1970s, with wide and generous caps and narrow elegant lowercase. McLauchlan started from a search for a mature typeface in the spirit of Franklin Gothic, Bell Gothic or Akzidenz-Grotesk.
summary: A grotesque-and-gothic fusion across six widths from Compressed to Extended, drawn from 1960s and 1970s conceptual art catalogue typography.
description: A wide-ranging grotesque by Seb McLauchlan that fuses the rationalised lines of Grotesk styles with the raw details of Gothic ones. ROM takes inspiration from typeface applications in 1960s and 1970s conceptual art catalogues; caps are wide and generous, lowercase narrow and elegant. Six subfamilies flow from Compressed to Extended (plus a Mono cut), each with seven weights and italics, and the O's counter shifts from diamond to rectangle to circle as widths grow. McLauchlan describes drawing it after searching unsuccessfully for a mature, full-weight Franklin Gothic, Bell Gothic or Akzidenz-Grotesk for his own publishing projects.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ROM, designed by their longstanding collaborator Seb McLauchlan, as a sturdy, confident fusion of classic Grotesk and Gothic typeface styles that combines the rationalised lines of the former with raw details from the latter. They note inspiration from typeface applications in conceptual art catalogues from the '60s and '70s, with proportions that are wide and generous in caps and narrow and elegant in lowercase. They explain that Seb is a graphic designer first who makes fonts second, and started ROM in search of a mature typeface in the spirit of Franklin Gothic, Bell Gothic or Akzidenz-Grotesk. ROM features six subfamilies that flow gracefully from Compressed to Extended, with the O's counter shifting from diamond to rectangle to circle. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Dinamo.

name: ABC Repro
designer: Erkin Karamemet
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Repro as a friendly, flexible sans serif inspired by signage and digital operating systems, merging clean design with complex font engineering and an overall geometric feel that is softer than mechanical neighbours.
summary: A friendly, geometric sans inspired by signage and operating systems, with nine weights, italics, a mono cut and an unusually deep set of OpenType features.
description: A geometric sans by Erkin Karamemet for Dinamo, drawn from signage and digital operating systems and shipped as a comprehensive toolkit. Repro spans nine weights with italics plus a Repro Mono variant, and ships as a customisable variable font with big system functionality and digital interfaces in mind. The OpenType set includes sharp and soft alternates, a 'Screen' weight calibrated for UI, square punctuation, plus a unique tool for circled emphases (numerals or letters inside circles) for visual hierarchy. Bold, reliable and recognisable, it sits between mechanical and personable.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Repro as a friendly, flexible sans serif inspired by signage and digital operating systems, merging clean design with complex font engineering. The extensive family includes nine weights with italics, a monospaced variant, and a wide range of OpenType features. As a customisable variable font, Repro is open-ended with big system functionality and digital interfaces in mind. They highlight the unique tool for circled emphases, sharp or soft alternates, web-specific glyphs, and a generally geometric feel that is softer and more personable than the mechanical typefaces it shares a community garden with. Designed by Erkin Karamemet, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Dinamo.

name: ABC Schengen
designer: Seb McLauchlan with Luke Charsley
classification_notes: Dinamo describe ABC Schengen as a multi-genre collection inspired by the Eurozone's visual language of cross-border, free-flowing logistics, manufacturing and construction. Soft Helvetica and boxy Eurostile sit in the background as 'spirit animals', with a width axis sliding between Schengen A, B and C; the monos lean closer to Aldo Novarese than Max Miedinger.
summary: A nine-family, vibes-based system across A/B/C widths plus Core, Line, Zone and three monos, riffing on Helvetica and Eurostile across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: An expansive system by Seb McLauchlan with Luke Charsley, planned and built over six years. ABC Schengen takes the Eurozone's logistics and infrastructure typography as cue and slides Schengen A, B and C along a shared width axis from soft Helvetica towards boxy Eurostile, with monospaced equivalents that lean closer to Aldo Novarese than Max Miedinger. Beyond the core trio, Schengen Zone adds chiselled serifs, Schengen Line smooths and tensions the geometry, and Schengen Core rebuilds on a smaller footprint. Nine weights with italics, all sharing structural dimensions, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek. Graphics by Johannes Breyer, Seb McLauchlan, Mimi Schmidl, Asel Tambay and Barnaby Mills; spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Schengen as a multi-genre collection of typefaces inspired by the Eurozone's visual language of cross-border, free-flowing logistics, manufacturing and construction, planned, developed and built over six years by Seb McLauchlan with Luke Charsley. At the centre are three connected families (Schengen A, B and C) with a shared width axis where Eurostile influence grows as characters widen, and monospaced equivalents that are closer to vibey Signor Novarese than straight-talking Herr Miedinger. Beyond the core families, Schengen Zone adds chiselled serifs, Schengen Line smooths and tensions geometry, and Schengen Core rebuilds at a smaller footprint. Covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek so it can speak all European languages of the many-tongued chain of transport. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Hugo Jourdan.

name: ABC Social
designer: Dinamo (Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Wei Huang, Malte Bentzen, Erkin Karamemet & Fabiola Mejia)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Social as a multi-people, multi-width mega collaboration; a friendly, smooth grotesque with variable font capabilities that fills a gap in their library, standing apart from their more constructed sans serifs both visually and technically.
summary: A friendly, smooth grotesque in four widths with eight weights, dynamic 's' and double-storey 'a', plus square, round and angled punctuation forms.
description: A grotesque sans by Dinamo (Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb, with Wei Huang, Malte Bentzen, Erkin Karamemet and Fabiola Mejia) that fills a gap in the foundry's library between its more constructed sans serifs. Social spans four widths (Social, Condensed, Extended, Mono) and eight weights from Hairline to Black with italics, packaged as variable fonts. Notable details include a freely designed dynamic 's', double-storey 'a', and three forms of punctuation: square, round and angled. Latin, Cyrillic and Greek are supported. Reach for it when work needs warmth and humour without losing grotesque discipline.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Social as a multi-people, multi-width mega collaboration made by many eyes and many players. A friendly, smooth grotesque with variable font capabilities, it fills a gap in their library, standing apart from their more constructed sans serifs both visually and technically. The name 'Social' nods to how the project came about and how designing it felt. They highlight a sense of humour with freely designed forms and rounded shapes, particularly the dynamic 's' and double storey 'a', and a 'g' that may become the most loved or hated character. Three forms of punctuation are included: square, round and angled. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Robert Janes.

name: ABC Solar
designer: Dinamo (Renan Rosatti)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Solar as a highly customizable geometric sans, sitting in the space between Remix and Homage of Kabel, Vogue, Metro No. 2, Johnston and Erbar-Grotesque plus Futura, with a dash of poetic licence.
summary: A customisable geometric sans positioned between Kabel, Futura and Johnston, with eight weights, italics and a Display companion for tight headlines.
description: A geometric sans by Dinamo's Renan Rosatti with a wide weight spectrum and matching italics. Solar has eight stylistic sets that reference geometric and humanistic typefaces from the early 20th century, allowing users to switch between straight and angled cuts. Defining details include almost-circular capital O, single-storey 'a' and a cross-like 't', balanced by open counters and straight terminals on 'c' and 's'. Two subfamilies sit in the system: Solar for legibility and functionality, and Solar Display for tight, punchy headlines. Latin Extended including Vietnamese is supported.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Solar as a highly customizable geometric sans with a large weight spectrum and matching italics. They frame it as 'giving Kabel, Vogue, Metro No. 2, Johnston and Erbar-Grotesque plus Futura', but call it what it is: living between Remix and Homage of all the above with a dash of poetic licence. Defining characteristics include round shapes such as an almost circular capital O, a single-storey 'a' and a cross-like 't', balanced by open counters and straight terminals in 'c' and 's'. Eight stylistic sets reference early 20th century geometric and humanistic typefaces. Solar Display is imagined for tight and punchy headlines. Designed and produced by Renan Rosatti, with creative direction by Fabian Harb and Johannes Breyer; spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.

name: ABC Stefan
designer: Stefan Marx, Johannes Breyer, Erkin Karamemet, Tanja Modraković
classification_notes: A direct digitisation of an artist's handwriting, with three stylistic subfamilies and a built-in randomiser to replicate natural inconsistency; classifies squarely as a handwriting script.
summary: A digitisation of Hamburg-based artist Stefan Marx's handwriting, featuring four alternates per character and a built-in randomiser that reproduces the natural inconsistency of writing by hand.
description: Each letter, figure and punctuation mark was drawn four times by Stefan Marx, and a randomiser built into the font software selects between variants as you type, avoiding the mechanical repetition that betrays most digital handwriting faces. The family splits into three subfamilies — Simple, Bubble and Bubble Tight — plus an emoji pack of standard Apple icons redrawn in Marx's hand. Lightning bolt swashes and a strikethrough mistake function add further personality.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Stefan as a digitisation of artist Stefan Marx's handwriting, built with four drawn alternates for every character and a randomiser function that displays them unpredictably to mimic the inconsistency of real handwriting. It comes in three subfamilies — Simple, Bubble and Bubble Tight — and includes an emoji pack of Apple icons rendered in Stefan's hand.

name: ABC Synt
designer: Kaj Lehmann
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Synt as a unitised reimagining of 19th-century modern faces complete with variable slant modulation and an emphatic rhythm, taking its logic and shapes from a typeface used in a 1900 book by influential commercial printer Theodore Low De Vinne.
summary: A unitised modern-face revival of a 1900 De Vinne typeface with three slant styles (16 deg, slanted 16 deg, Turbo 32 deg) for streaming-era display.
description: A modern face by Kaj Lehmann for Dinamo, drawn from a typeface used in a 1900 book by commercial printer Theodore Low De Vinne and reframed for apps, podcasting and streaming. Synt's parametric rhythm holds an identical distance between and within every stem, and the family ships with six weights, a Mono cut and three styles of italics: True Italics (16 degrees), a Slanted variation (16 degrees), and a Turbo Slant at 32 degrees. The variable font slides through the full slant range. Stylistic sets cover Schoolbook alternates, Beethoven alternates (b, d, q, p), centred dieresis, alternate '@', and circled numbers.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Synt as a unitised reimagining of 19th-century modern faces complete with variable slant modulation and an emphatic rhythm, taking its logic and shapes from a typeface used in a 1900 book by influential commercial printer Theodore Low De Vinne, remixed with special glyphs and musical alternates ideal for apps, podcasting and streaming. The 'parametric' rhythm means an identical distance between and within every stem. The family includes six weights, a mono cut, and three styles of italics: True Italics (16 deg), Slanted (16 deg) and Turbo Slant (32 deg). Designed by Kaj Lehmann, production by Renan Rosatti.

name: ABC Walter
designer: Dinamo (Renan Rosatti, Arnaud Chemin) & Omnigroup (Simon Mager & Leonardo Azzolini)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Walter as a long-standing scholarly revival not based on a pre-existing typeface but on a tool used to teach students how to draw letterforms, designed by Swiss type educator Walter Kach in the mid-twentieth century. Walter Alte is a faithful study of Kach's lessons; Walter Neue is an extension and update.
optical_sizes_or_widths: Walter Alte (Rauchwaren, Normalgrotesk, Roentgentherapie, Stahlbau, Plakat)
summary: A scholarly revival of Walter Kach's mid-century lettering teaching tool, in faithful Walter Alte cuts plus an extended Walter Neue family with mono.
description: A Swiss grotesque revival by Dinamo (Renan Rosatti, Arnaud Chemin) and Omnigroup (Simon Mager and Leonardo Azzolini), built not from a finished typeface but from Walter Kach's mid-twentieth-century lettering teaching tool. Walter Alte is a faithful study of Kach's lessons in cuts including Rauchwaren, Normalgrotesk, Roentgentherapie, Stahlbau and Plakat; Walter Alte Roentgentherapie carries the highest contrast, Normalgrotesk is the most constructed and mono-linear, and Rauchwaren sits in between, recalling the way Kach's work fertilised the Helvetica empire. Walter Neue extends Kach's teachings into a fourteen-style family with seven weights, italics and stylistic alternates, plus Walter Neue Mono; high contrast appears only in the heaviest weights, with rounded dots and friendlier punctuation.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Walter as a long-standing, joint scholarly effort by Dinamo and Omnigroup, comprising Walter Alte and Walter Neue, two related yet ideologically opposed families celebrating an author and design educator with fingerprints all over Switzerland's most famous fonts. The point of departure is a tool used to teach students how to draw letterforms, designed by type educator Walter Kach in the mid-twentieth century. Walter Alte is a faithful study of Kach's lessons in three cuts (Roentgentherapie, Normalgrotesk, Rauchwaren), while Walter Neue is an extension, update and digitally-minded interpretation in fourteen styles, seven weights, italics and stylistic alternates, with rounded dots on i, j, a, o, u and friendlier punctuation. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Dinamo.

name: ABC Whyte
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb & Johannes Breyer, with Erkin Karamemet & Fabiola Mejia)
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Whyte as a contemporary grotesque typeface filled with idiosyncrasies and character. Whyte has smooth and sharp transitions; Whyte Inktrap has ink traps at its joints, with variable-axis control to deepen or shorten them.
summary: A contemporary grotesque in six subfamilies including a variable Inktrap axis, mono and semi-mono cuts, across Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Hangul and Hebrew.
description: A contemporary grotesque by Dinamo (Fabian Harb and Johannes Breyer, with Erkin Karamemet and Fabiola Mejia) with idiosyncrasies and character. The system splits into Whyte (smooth and sharp transitions) and Whyte Inktrap (ink traps at joints), and via variable font technology users can deepen or shorten the ink traps for the first time, sliding from deep wells to shorter cuts. Six subfamilies cover Whyte, Inktrap, Mono, Mono Inktrap, Semi-Mono and Semi-Mono Inktrap, each with ten weights from Thin to Super and matching italics. Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Hangul and Hebrew are supported, with Hangul, Japanese and Chinese pairings recommended.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Whyte as a contemporary grotesque typeface filled with idiosyncrasies and character, available as two distinctive subfamilies. ABC Whyte has smooth and sharp transitions, while ABC Whyte Inktrap has ink traps at its joints, originally a hot metal printing necessity. Using variable font technology, users can deepen or shorten the ink traps for the first time, sliding from deep wells to shorter cuts, transforming something once functional into a beautiful design detail in its own right. Designed by Fabian Harb and Johannes Breyer with Erkin Karamemet and Fabiola Mejia, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, production by Robert Janes and Chi Long Trieu.

name: Arizona
designer: Elias Hanzer with Alex Lescieux
classification_notes: Arizona spans five stylistic sub-families from high-contrast Renaissance-influenced serif to humanist grotesque sans, making it genuinely cross-categorical; classified here as serif because that marks one pole of its design range, though it is most accurately a superfamily variable font.
summary: A variable superfamily by Elias Hanzer and Alex Lescieux that packages five stylistic registers — from high-contrast serif to humanist grotesque — into a single font file.
description: Arizona runs from a high-contrast, Renaissance-inflected serif at one extreme to a clean grotesque sans at the other, with Flare, Mix and Text occupying the hybrid territory between them. Each of the five sub-families comes in three widths and five weights with italics throughout, all housed in a single variable font file. The family has been used by the San Francisco Symphony, Glyphs and the Fashion Research Library, covering a range from fine editorial typesetting to large-scale display work.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Arizona as the first ever sans-to-serif superfamily, packaging five distinct looks — Serif, Text, Mix, Flare and Sans — into one slim, all-genres-in-one font file. Arizona Serif features high-contrast, pointy forms with a Renaissance-meets-modern feel, while Arizona Sans is a straightforward grotesque with a humanist touch; Flare, Mix and Text occupy the range in between. Each subfamily comes in three widths and five weights plus italics, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini and production by Hugo Jourdan at Dinamo.

name: Asfalt
designer: Fabian Harb, Olga Umpeleva
classification_notes: Rooted in painted road lettering and mid-century stencil lettering, Asfalt's elongated, vertically stretched forms make it a display face with a functional vernacular origin rather than a conventional text or grotesque classification.
summary: Drawn from painted road markings and a mid-century Niggli stencil source, this display family stretches across five widths and four weights to honour the perspective-compensating geometry of street typography.
description: Asfalt takes its formal cues from the vertically elongated letterforms painted on road surfaces, designed to read as correctly proportioned from the low angle of an approaching driver. A stencilled road lettering specimen from Niggli's mid-century Lettera series and a Brazilian road-signage manual found in a Rio de Janeiro thrift shop anchored the design process. Five width variants from Compressed to Expanded, each in four weights, give the family considerable range across poster work, editorial design and identity systems where a strong vernacular character is needed.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Asfalt as drawing from the design of painted road letters, which are stretched vertically to compensate for the low perspective of an approaching driver, so that words like STOP and GO read as perfectly compact from behind the wheel. The starting point was a stencilled version of road lettering from Niggli's mid-century Lettera series, alongside a road surface signage design manual found by Fabian in a Rio de Janeiro thrift store. The family spans Compressed, Condensed, Normal, Extended and Expanded widths, each across Light, Regular, Medium and Bold weights, with a variable version also available.

name: Bingo
designer: Johannes Breyer
classification_notes: Derived from American wood type specimens, with polygon-drawn inner and outer contours that produce a rough, blocky character at large sizes — firmly in the display category with a vernacular wood type lineage.
summary: A polygon-contoured wood type revival by Johannes Breyer, first released in 2011 and based on a specimen from Rob Roy Kelly's American Wood Type.
description: Drawn with polygonal inner and outer contours, the letterforms appear smooth at small sizes but reveal an angular, brutal structure when set large. The design traces back to a sample in Rob Roy Kelly's American Wood Type: 1829–1900 and was Breyer's first typeface, released while interning at NORM via a purpose-built website. A second set of monolinear, thin punctuation was added in 2012, and the family includes a condensed variant.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Bingo as rough on the outside and soft on the inside, with an edgy exterior contrasting against sympathetic, rounded inner contours. It was Johannes Breyer's first typeface, released in 2011 while he was an intern at NORM, and is based on a specimen from Rob Roy Kelly's American Wood Type: 1829–1900. Unlike its source, the contours are drawn with polygons so that the face reads smooth at small scale and brutal at large sizes. A second set of monolinear thin punctuation was added in 2012, with production handled by Renan Rosatti.

name: Bubblegum
designer: Fabian Harb, Michelangelo Nigra, Marc Rouault
classification_notes: Bubblegum is a geometric, modular display face with rounded, inflated forms characteristic of the bubbly genre, but with a more refined underlying logic and small caps baked in. Its playful character and extreme roundness place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A geometric, highly modular display face from Dinamo that pushes the bubbly genre further with integrated small caps and a monospaced companion.
description: Designed by Fabian Harb, Michelangelo Nigra and Marc Rouault at Dinamo, it fuses soft rounded forms with a strict geometric modularity that separates it from looser bubbly typefaces. Small caps are built directly into the family, an unusual feature for this genre that opens up new typographic possibilities. A monospaced companion, Bubblegum Mono, extends the family towards functional use cases without abandoning its distinctive character.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Bubblegum as soft and silly but also geometric and highly modular, calling it a sticky clash of two extremes. They note it has a refined logic distinguishing it from the typical bubbly designs found on free font sites, and highlight small caps as a feature that brings something new to the genre. A monospaced companion is offered for those who want to use it in more practical settings.

name: Camera
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejía, Sascha Bente
classification_notes: Camera is described as a neutral, toned-down grotesque similar in style to Helvetica but with freer proportions and a narrower body, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A grotesque sans built around scaled-up light traps, the visual compensation technique used on low-resolution TV screens in the 60s and 70s, offered with and without its signature holes.
description: Conceived by Fabian Harb around the time of Dinamo's founding, Camera reinterprets the light trap as a deliberate design feature rather than a technical workaround, punching visible holes into letterforms in the manner of Swiss Emmental cheese. A Plain variant strips the holes entirely, yielding a neutral grotesque with slightly freer proportions and a narrower body than Helvetica. The family runs across five weights with italics, and extends into Camera Mono and Camera Plain Mono, with a variable font also available.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Camera as a reinterpretation of light traps, the low-resolution compensation technique used in 60s and 70s television typography. They scaled the traps up into visible holes, and also offer the design without them as Camera Plain. The font is credited as an early work of co-founder Fabian Harb, and has been among the most requested typefaces in Dinamo's inbox. Design credits include Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejía and Sascha Bente, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.

name: Camera Rounded
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejía, Sascha Bente
classification_notes: A neutral grotesque with rounded terminals, described by Dinamo as similar in style to Helvetica but with freer proportions. The rounded variant softens the geometric rigour of the base Camera family, which was derived from television light traps.
summary: A rounded grotesque derived from ABC Camera, itself a reinterpretation of television light traps from the 1960s and 70s, available in four subfamilies including mono and plain variants.
description: The Camera Rounded family softens the distinctive hole-cut characters of the base Camera design, which drew on the light traps used to improve text legibility on low-resolution TV screens. Five weights with italics run across four subfamilies: the standard Rounded, a monospaced variant, and Plain versions of each that remove the holes entirely. Proportions sit narrower than Helvetica with less strictly defined geometry, and a variable font is included covering weight and slant axes.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Camera as a reinterpretation of light traps, a device used in 1960s and 70s television typography to compensate for low screen resolution. The rounded variant scales up these features as visible holes (likened to Swiss Emmental cheese), while the Plain versions remove them entirely.
