The design is credited as an early work of co-founder Fabian Harb, conceptualised around the time of Dinamo's inception, and was one of their most-requested typefaces prior to release.

name: Connect
designer: Fabian Harb, Louis Brousseaud, Frédéric Jaman, Johannes Breyer, Oliver-Selim Boualam, Lukas Marstaller
classification_notes: Built on a dot-grid system with screw-head-shaped dots, Connect is a display face defined entirely by its modular, grid-based construction. Its conceptual character and novelty place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A dot font built on a grid of connector shapes, offered in five screw-head styles and claimed to be the world's first italic dot font.
description: Designed by a team at Dinamo, Connect uses a grid of dots shaped like common screw heads — Flat, Hex, Nail, Phillips and Torx — as its constructive unit. It comes in both proportional and monospaced versions, each with corresponding italics, making it an unusual entry in the dot-font genre. The family covers Latin Extended and Greek, with small caps, fractions and a full suite of symbols.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Connect as a digital dot font based on the ABC C-o-n-n-e-c-t-o-r-s grid, containing both monospaced and proportional styles with corresponding italics, which they claim makes it probably the world's first italic dot font. It comes in five screw head variants — Flat, Torx, Hex, Nail and Phillips — designed by Fabian Harb, Louis Brousseaud, Frédéric Jaman, Johannes Breyer, Oliver-Selim Boualam and Lukas Marstaller.

name: Cowboy
classification_notes: A self-described western-style slab serif with Dinamo's signature butterfly serifs, placing it firmly in the display slab tradition associated with American woodtype and frontier typography.
summary: A western-style slab serif from Dinamo that takes a genre usually left to under-extended free fonts and builds it out with 643 glyphs, 11,217 kerning pairs and coverage for over 127 languages.
description: Where most western slabs are poorly kerned free-font afterthoughts, this one is built with Dinamo's characteristic rigour: butterfly serifs, no alternate letterforms, and a glyph set that includes subscripts, superscripts and arbitrary fractions. The 397 accented characters extend coverage to over 127 languages, which is unusually thorough for a display typeface of this genre. Only a single weight is offered, and the no-mercy, no-alternates approach is very much by design.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Cowboy as a straightforward western-style slab serif in a genre they note is generally overlooked and more often than not a poorly produced, under-extended free font download. They position Cowboy as the exception, citing 643 glyphs balanced by 11,217 kerning pairs, 397 accented characters covering over 127 languages, and typographic niceties such as subscripts, superscripts and arbitrary fractions. It features Dinamo-style butterfly serifs and offers zero alternate letterforms — no mercy out west, as they put it.

name: Diatype
designer: Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti, Andree Paat, Đức Cao
classification_notes: Dinamo describe it as a warm yet sharp grotesque informed by pre-digital Diatype typesetting machines and the Swiss Neo-grotesque tradition, placing it squarely in the grotesque sans-serif lineage.
summary: A warm, screen-optimised grotesque from Dinamo, spanning seven width variants, a mono cut and ten script systems from Arabic to Hangul.
description: Taking its name from the pre-digital Diatype typesetting machine, this grotesque blends Swiss Neo-grotesque structure with a warmth suited to extended on-screen reading. Eight weights from Thin to Ultra run across upright and italic in every subfamily, with the family stretching from Compressed through to Extended plus dedicated Mono and Semi Mono cuts. Script coverage is unusually broad for a grotesque, reaching Arabic, Armenian, Devanagari, Cyrillic, Georgian, Greek, Hebrew, Hangul and Thai alongside Latin.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Diatype as a warm yet sharp grotesque ideal for text and reading on screen, with its name referencing the clunky pre-digital typesetting machines that shaped both its letterforms and the Swiss Neo-grotesque genre more broadly.

name: Diatype Extra
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti, Andree Paat
classification_notes: Diatype Extra comprises monospaced and semi-monospaced compressed and condensed subfamilies derived from the Diatype grotesque, placing it firmly in the monospaced grotesque category with strong Swiss Neo-grotesque roots.
summary: An extended family of monospaced and semi-monospaced compressed and condensed cuts grown from Dinamo's Diatype grotesque, designed for screen reading.
description: Diatype Extra expands the Diatype system into four tightly drawn subfamilies: Mono Compressed, Mono Condensed, Semi Mono Compressed and Semi Mono Condensed. Each runs across five weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics, and variable font versions are available. The parent typeface draws its character from pre-digital diatype typesetting machines and the Swiss Neo-grotesque tradition, giving these compressed cuts warmth alongside their technical precision.
foundry_blurb: Diatype is described by Dinamo as a warm yet sharp grotesque suited to text and screen reading, with its name referencing the clunky pre-digital typesetting machines that shaped its letterforms and informed the Swiss Neo-grotesque genre. Diatype Extra extends this into monospaced and semi-monospaced compressed and condensed families, designed by Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb alongside Elias Hanzer, Erkin Karamemet, Renan Rosatti and Andree Paat, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.

name: Diatype Rounded
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Elias Hanzer, Renan Rosatti, Arnaud Chemin
classification_notes: Rooted in the Swiss Neo-grotesque tradition, Diatype Rounded applies uniform terminal rounding that gives the genre an approachable, mechanical quality rather than the usual clinical neutrality.
summary: A rounded Neo-grotesque with an intentionally mechanical warmth, extended into mono and semi-mono variants across eight weights.
description: Built on Swiss Neo-grotesque bones, each letterform has been individually buffered to produce terminals that read as though engraved with a single continuous tool stroke. The family spans eight weights from Thin to Ultra with matching italics, and branches into Diatype Rounded Mono and Semi Mono for tabular and coding contexts. Numerous OpenType alternates including capital G, lowercase a, q, t and R allow fine-tuning of the tone from clean to slightly playful.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Diatype Rounded as a warm, curvy sans with friendly shapes informed by the Swiss Neo-grotesque genre and pre-digital typesetting machines. They note the overall vibe is bold, a little pop, with an understated sense of humor, and that the rounded forms feel oddly mechanical, as if each character has been engraved with a chunky tool in one continuous line. Each character was buffered individually.

name: Estragon
designer: Fabian Harb, Daniel Chessari
classification_notes: Estragon is rooted in an 1890s ornamental wood-type specimen, redrawn with alternate stylistic sets including Wildstyle, Arrow and Bubble variants, placing it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: A revival of an obscure 1890s ornamental wood-type specimen, redrawn by Dinamo in 2017 with alternate Wildstyle, Arrow and Bubble character sets that can be mixed freely.
description: Drawn from a single spread in Harrild & Sons' circa-1890 wood-letter specimen book, Estragon was rebuilt for contemporary use across six weights with a variable axis. Three supplementary stylistic sets — Wildstyle, Arrow and Bubble — allow individual characters to be swapped and combined, giving the family an unusually modular expressive range. Sam de Groot's description of it as 'Schwitters-meets-Wildstyle', coined for its first use in the Condo New York identity, captures the tension between its historical origins and its graphic energy.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo discovered Estragon's source material while scrolling the Type Foundries Archive Twitter account, identifying a spread from Harrild & Sons' New & Abridged Specimens of Wood-letter (circa 1890). Unable to find further characters or historical context, they redrew the design in 2017 for contemporary use, adding three distinctive styles — Wildstyle, Arrow and Bubble — that can be freely combined at the level of individual characters. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini.

name: Favorit
designer: Johannes Breyer & Fabian Harb, with Erkin Karamemet, Immo Schneider, Arnaud Chemin, Ruggero Magrì
classification_notes: A low-contrast grotesque with geometric construction and subtle idiosyncratic details, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with humanist inflections.
summary: A low-contrast grotesque from Dinamo with geometric rigidity offset by deliberate oddities, available across seven sub-families and ten scripts.
description: Dinamo's Favorit pairs clean geometric structure with subtle quirks — alternate forms for the ampersand, R, a and y among them — giving it personality without eccentricity. The family spans eight weights with italics throughout, extends into Compressed, Condensed, Expanded and Extended widths, and includes Favorit Lining, a variant with a smart built-in underline that merges descenders and connects words. Global coverage reaches ten scripts including Arabic, Hangul and Devanagari, and a variable font version handles slant, width and weight in a single file.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Favorit as a straightforward, low-contrast grotesque that blends geometric rigidity with subtle oddities and a humorous touch. Available in multiple widths and a monospaced variant, it also includes Favorit Lining — their younger yet much smarter sibling — which features a built-in smart underline that can merge descending characters and conjoin words in Connected mode. The family has been extended over several years to cover additional scripts including Cyrillic, Greek, Hangul and Arabic.

name: Gaisyr
designer: Fabian Harb, Michelangelo Nigra
classification_notes: Gaisyr features distinctive 'Butterfly Serifs' rooted in early 18th-century geometric constructions by Jacques Jaugeon, blending strict geometric underpinnings with calligraphic gestures — placing it closest to a transitional serif with display sensibilities.
summary: A serif with Dinamo's signature Butterfly Serifs, drawing on Jacques Jaugeon's early 18th-century royal geometric type sketches across proportional, semi-mono and mono variants.
description: Designed by Fabian Harb with Michelangelo Nigra, Gaisyr holds a deliberate tension between strict geometric construction and loose, hand-held letter forms. The family spans seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and comes in three spacing variants: proportional, semi-monospaced and fully monospaced. Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage, plus OpenType features including swashes, discretionary ligatures and small caps, make it unusually well-equipped for editorial and international work.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Gaisyr as a beautiful typeface featuring their house-style Butterfly Serifs, taking its point de départ from sketches by Jacques Jaugeon, the early 18th-century royal typographer for King Louis XIV. They note a tension between strict geometry and loopy, hand-held gestures living on in its elegant forms, and offer it in proportional, monospaced and semi-monospaced styles.

name: Galapagos
designer: Felix Salut, Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb
classification_notes: A modular display typeface built from interchangeable geometric building blocks, with characters that evolve across three distinct style axes (rounded, straight, angular) and their hybrids, making it primarily a display or experimental category entry.
summary: A modular typeface built from interchangeable letterform components across three style axes and their hybrids, derived from Felix Salut's physical block-based drawing game.
description: Emerging from 42 distinct cuts, Galapagos organises its characters across rounded, straight and angular styles, with hybrids combining any two or three of these axes. Each cut is available in five weights and in grid and non-grid versions, producing a sprawling system of 70 subfamilies in total. Stylistic alternates allow individual characters to shift position along a vertical grid, giving designers direct control over the letterform sequences within a composition.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Galapagos as a typeface whose characters evolve from one another, analogous to the unique species of the Galápagos Islands. It originated from designer Felix Salut's Galapagos Game, a physical set of building blocks for drawing letter shapes, which Dinamo transformed into a starkly futuristic yet subtly biological font system of 42 different cuts.

name: Ginto
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Ginto as a geometric-humanist typeface, drawing on strict Modernist geometry and the more baroque, animated styles of phototypesetting-era sans-serifs from the 1950s and 60s.
summary: A geometric-humanist sans by Seb McLauchlan that plays strict Modernist geometry against the animated, baroque qualities of 1950s and 60s phototypesetting-era types.
description: Designed by London-based graphic designer Seb McLauchlan, Ginto sits at the intersection of rigorous geometric construction and the looser, more expressive letterforms that emerged during the phototypesetting period. The family spans four sub-families — Ginto, Ginto Condensed, Ginto Nord and Ginto Nord Condensed — each running from Hairline to Ultra across full italics, with Nord extending the x-height and character width for greater visual impact. Script coverage reaches Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic, and a variable font is available across all sub-families.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Ginto as an exuberant geometric-humanist typeface built on the tension between circular and rectangular forms. Designer Seb McLauchlan developed the font while researching the shift from strict Modernist purity to the more baroque, animated styles of the phototypesetting era, remixing those two historic impulses into a dynamic and charismatic set of forms. ABC Ginto Nord amplifies that tone, expanding the x-height and character width and stretching from Hairline to Ultra Bold.

name: Ginto Rounded
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Rounded terminals on a geometric-humanist base draw from circular Futura-adjacent proportion models tempered by softer, more animated 1960s–70s forms, placing it squarely in the geometric humanist sans category with a rounded variant identity.
summary: A rounded geometric-humanist sans by Seb McLauchlan that balances soft terminals with Futura-like proportion models across three subfamilies.
description: Drawn with rounded terminals and an interplay between circular geometry and humanist warmth, the family takes cues from animated graphic styles of the 1960s and 70s. Standard Ginto Rounded suits smaller text with a compact lowercase, while Ginto Rounded Nord offers a taller x-height and wider characters for display use; Ginto Rounded Nord Condensed handles tighter settings. A variable font bridges the Nord widths, and the family ships with eight weights plus italics throughout.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Ginto Rounded as a geometric-humanist font of soft edges and playful forms, exploring tension between circular shapes and moments of sharpness. They liken it to a field recording capturing baroque, animated 1960s–70s styles in dialogue with stricter modern proportion models such as Futura. The standard cut suits smaller text while Nord is built for chunky display use, and a variable font allows fine-tuning across widths.

name: Gramercy
designer: Robert Janes
classification_notes: A contemporary serif with optical size subfamilies (Standard, Fine, Display) and variable axes for contrast and weight. Painterly flared strokes and uppercase swashes give it an expressive editorial character informed by Schneidler's Amalthea.
summary: A 48-style contemporary serif by Robert Janes, spanning three optical subfamilies with uppercase swashes and variable axes for weight and contrast.
description: Designed by Robert Janes and spaced by Igino Marini, Gramercy draws from F.H. Ernst Schneidler's Amalthea (1956) as its primary reference, translating that influence into overhanging details and flared strokes where another typeface would use ball terminals. The family runs across Standard, Fine and Display subfamilies, each covering eight weights from Thin to Super with corresponding italics and uppercase swashes. Variable font files allow continuous movement across both the contrast and weight axes, with Latin, Cyrillic and Greek script support throughout.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Gramercy as an elegant, contemporary serif that balances slightly painterly and whimsical forms with functionality and sturdiness. With 48 styles across three subfamilies — Standard, Fine and Display — each comes with uppercase swashes across all weights. The typeface was initially informed by F.H. Ernst Schneidler's Amalthea (1956), and its variable font files allow movement across both contrast and weight axes.

name: Gravity
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Robert Janes, Fabiola Mejía, Olga Umpeleva
classification_notes: A single-weight, ultra-heavy headline grotesque with 12 width variants spanning XXXX Compressed to Wide, inspired by heavy grotesques of the 1960s and 70s. Its extreme width range and baseline-manipulation features place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A one-weight headline grotesque in 12 widths, from XXXX Compressed to Wide, with extensive OpenType features for stacking and baseline manipulation.
description: Built on a skeleton drawn from heavy grotesques of the 1960s and 70s, this display typeface spans an extreme range of widths available as individual styles or as a single variable font file. Stylistic alternates allow characters to jump over the baseline or hang from the top, enabling unusually compact and playful title arrangements. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini, and Cyrillic support is available as a companion release.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Gravity as a one-weight, 12-width headline typeface whose initial skeleton was inspired by heavy grotesques from the 1960s and 70s, stretched and compressed into extreme widths. Its many stylistic alternates allow playful settings and super-compact title arrangements, including features that let you make characters jump over the baseline or hang from the top. All 12 styles can be used separately or as a single variable font file.

name: Helveesti
designer: Dinamo (Fabian Harb, Robert Janes, Johannes Breyer, Fabiola Mejía) with Andree Paat
classification_notes: A digitisation of a Soviet-era Helvetica clone, retaining its phototypesetting-era ink-trap spikes as a defining characteristic; sits firmly in the grotesque sans tradition descended from Helvetica.
summary: A digitisation of a Soviet-era Helvetica clone discovered in an Estonian children's book, distinguished by its phototypesetting ink-trap spikes.
description: Found by Fabian Harb while teaching in Tallinn in 2014, the source typeface was a Soviet-era Helvetica imitation printed in a vintage children's book, complete with spiked ink traps at stroke junctions designed to compensate for phototypesetting bleed. Dinamo digitised and expanded it into eight weights with italics, offering two subfamilies: Helveesti with the spikes smoothed away, and Helveesti Spikes retaining the original mutant corners. Spacing and kerning by Igino Marini; a variable version, Helveesti Plus Variable, is also available.
foundry_blurb: During a type-teaching residency in Tallinn in winter 2014, Fabian Harb came across a Soviet-era Helvetica clone in a secondhand Estonian children's book. Its ink traps, which flare outward like mutated tails, were a functional response to phototypesetting blur. With the full alphabet printed at the back of the book, Dinamo had everything needed to begin digitisation. The family is available in two flavours — Helveesti and Helveesti Spikes — each in eight weights with italics, plus a variable version.

name: Honeymoon
designer: Fabian Harb, Arnaud Chemin
classification_notes: Honeymoon is explicitly described as a monospaced script typeface, combining connected letterforms typical of script with a fixed-width grid, an unusual structural combination that Dinamo frames as a deliberate design conceit.
summary: A monospaced script family from Dinamo with a romantic character, spanning seven weights and loaded with swashes, alternate caps and celebratory ornaments.
description: Unusual in its construction, Honeymoon places a flowing script onto a fixed monospaced grid, a constraint that gives the family a distinct rhythm across its seven weights from Hairline to Bold. OpenType features include single-width uppercase alternates, non-connected o and s forms, swash symbols, oldstyle figures and double-width numerals. A companion subfamily, Honeymoon Mix, offers a Regular cut with alternate letterforms, and the full family is available as a variable font.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Honeymoon as a true Dinamo-style monospaced script typeface made for friends and lovers, noting romantic features such as single-width uppercase alternates, extravagant swashes and other celebratory alternates. Design is credited to Fabian Harb and Arnaud Chemin, with production by Hugo Jourdan.

name: Ikarus
designer: Fabian Harb, Erkin Karamemet, Johannes Breyer
classification_notes: The monolinear subfamilies are humanist sans-serifs rooted in wayfinding with open terminals and clearly distinctive letterforms; the Contrast subfamilies introduce high-contrast strokes for display use, extending the family into a dual-register system.
summary: A large humanist family by Dinamo built around wayfinding principles, pairing a monolinear text design with a high-contrast display companion across three widths, eight weights and matching italics.
description: Designed by Fabian Harb, Erkin Karamemet and Johannes Breyer at Dinamo, Ikarus pairs a sturdy monolinear text design with an articulate high-contrast companion for headlines, the two sharing open terminals and clearly distinctive letterforms drawn for readability at distance and in poor light. Nine subfamilies span Condensed, Normal and Extended widths, each running eight weights from Thin to Ultra with matching italics, and the whole system is tied together by a variable font with axes for weight, width, slant and contrast. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Ikarus as a humanist double-family: a sturdy monolinear text design on one side and an articulate, high-contrast companion for headlines and display on the other. Rooted in wayfinding, it features open terminals and clearly distinctive letterforms optimised for readability at distance, in challenging light conditions and at the smallest type sizes. Three widths, eight weights per subfamily and matching italics give it serious range, with a variable font axis allowing movement across all of Ikarus' extremes.

name: Laica
designer: Alessio D'Ellena
classification_notes: Laica blends broad-nib and pointed-nib calligraphic influences into a sans-serif with chiselled transitions, giving it a humanist warmth distinct from purely geometric or grotesque models. The proportional and monospaced subfamilies share the same calligraphic underpinning.
summary: A humanist sans built from the friction between broad-nib and pointed-nib drawing tools, sketched on trams and in airports by Alessio D'Ellena during his time at the Royal Academy of Art in The Hague.
description: Designed by Alessio D'Ellena, Laica uses chiselled stroke transitions by default to create optical rhythm across text setting, with a stylistic set offering cleaner, straight-jointed alternates for a more restrained tone. Both the proportional and monospaced subfamilies run across five weights from Light to Bold with matching italics. The calligraphic origins give the letterforms an energy that sits between the rigour of a text face and the looseness of hand-drawn type.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Laica as the product of a forced collaboration between the broad nib and pointed nib pen, two common but fundamentally different drawing tools. Designed by Alessio D'Ellena during student life at the Royal Academy of Art in The Hague, often in airport armchairs or on Rome trams, its patchwork of shapes is framed as a playful retort to calligraphic tradition and a reflection of cheap flights, constant hustle and remote work.

name: Marfa
designer: Fabian Harb, Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Marfa draws directly from early American gothic typefaces of the hot metal era, placing it firmly in the grotesque tradition. Its utilitarian structure, ink-trap sensibility and monolinear rhythm are characteristic of 19th-century American gothics.
summary: A 28-style grotesque family rooted in early American gothic typefaces, extended from a three-weight workhorse into a full family with matching italics, mono and semi-mono variants.
description: Originally drawn by Fabian Harb as a three-weight utilitarian face called Gothic No. 6, the family was later expanded with Seb McLauchlan adding italics, small caps, old-style figures and a mono counterpart. The 28 styles span Thin to Black across three subfamilies: Marfa, Marfa Mono and Marfa Semi Mono, all available as variable fonts. Spacing and kerning were handled by Igino Marini, and the family has seen use across cultural institutions, galleries and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Marfa as stylistically born in the hot metal typesetting period but drawn at speed, with the uprights completed in under 1,000 hours and the italics in less than half that. Originally a three-weight typeface called Gothic No. 6, the family grew to 28 styles through a collaboration between Fabian Harb and Seb McLauchlan, with small caps and old-style figures added as a nod to late 19th-century printing practice.

name: Marist
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: Marist draws directly from Old Style tradition, referencing Nicolas Jenson's 15th-century roman and William Morris's Golden Type revival. Prominent serifs, wide uppercase proportions and humanist construction firmly place it in the Old Style subcategory.
summary: An Old Style serif by Seb McLauchlan, tracing a line from Jenson's 15th-century Venetian roman through William Morris's Golden Type to contemporary screen and print use.
description: Designed by Seb McLauchlan with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, Marist takes its cues from two landmarks in Old Style type: Nicolas Jenson's roman, cut in 15th-century Venice for its legibility, and Morris's Golden Type, which reinterpreted Jenson as a rejection of industrial printing. The family runs across six weights from Book to Black, each with a matching italic, plus a variable italic axis. Wide uppercase proportions and prominent serifs give it warmth and readability suited to long-form editorial and book work.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Marist as a warm, reader-friendly typeface with prominent serifs and wide uppercase proportions. Its forms were developed through archival research into the Old Style genre, with particular attention to Jenson's roman from 15th-century Venice and Morris's Golden Type from 19th-century London. The design fine-tunes those historical sources for contemporary eyes and digital libraries.

name: Monument Grotesk
designer: Kasper-Florio (Larissa Kasper & Rosario Florio), Robert Janes, Fabiola Mejía
classification_notes: Rooted in a Palmer & Rey 1884 specimen, the design retains the compact skeleton and sharp end strokes of a 19th-century grotesque, digitised and reinterpreted for contemporary use with idiosyncratic, deliberately unpolished character.
summary: A grotesque rooted in an 1884 Palmer & Rey specimen, reinterpreted by Kasper-Florio and Dinamo with a raw, compact character that has since become a fixture in contemporary editorial and identity work.
description: Originating from a scan of an 1884 Palmer & Rey specimen book, the design was digitised and reinterpreted by Swiss studio Kasper-Florio before being released publicly through Dinamo in 2018. Its compact skeleton, high vertical contrast and sharp end strokes give it a distinctively unrefined quality that reads as confident rather than rough. The family spans eight weights with italics, and extends into Mono and Semi-Mono cuts, with coverage across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek scripts.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Monument Grotesk as emerging from a Palmer & Rey 1884 specimen that Kasper-Florio found online, drawn to its compact skeleton, high vertical contrast and sharp end strokes. Digitised and reinterpreted for contemporary use, it became the studio's house typeface before Dinamo made it publicly available in 2018. They characterise it as honest, unrefined and idiosyncratic, with a raw and pleasingly unpolished feel.

name: Oracle
designer: Johannes Breyer, Andree Paat, Thy Hà, Chi-Long Trieu, Daria Cohen
classification_notes: Oracle is described as an elegant sans serif with friendly, smooth contours and subtle contrast, placing it in the humanist sans tradition. Oracle Triple takes an experimental approach to monospaced rhythm but shares the same origins.
summary: A two-family release from Dinamo pairing an elegant, contrast-rich sans across nine weights with Oracle Triple, an oddity that applies monospaced-style bounding box logic in units of thirds.
description: Oracle and Oracle Triple share an origin but diverge sharply in intention. The main family offers nine weights from Thin to Ultra with matching italics, smooth contours, subtle contrast and a wide range of alternates and stylistic sets for varied typographic expression. Oracle Triple takes the logic of monospaced letterforms to an extreme, dividing each character's bounding box into thirds and letting different glyphs occupy varying numbers of units, resulting in an idiosyncratic rhythmic cadence. Multiscript coverage extends to Greek and Cyrillic, with spacing and kerning handled by Igino Marini.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Oracle as two families that began in the same place but wandered off in entirely different directions. The primary family is an elegant sans serif with friendly, smooth contours and subtle contrast, offering a high degree of customisability through alternates and stylistic sets. Oracle Triple applies the logic of monospaced letterforms to an extreme, segmenting the bounding box into thirds so that different characters occupy differing numbers of units.

name: Paloma
designer: Fabian Harb, Sascha Bente, Jovana Jocic
classification_notes: Paloma reduces letterforms to their most elemental geometric constituents — triangle, circle, square — following the constructivist tradition of Bayer and Tschichold. Its highly stylised forms place it firmly in the display category despite covering a broad weight range.
summary: A constructivist-derived geometric typeface by Dinamo, reducing letterforms to the triangle, circle and square across seven weights with a variable axis.
description: First drawn in 2021 for the Bikini Books visual identity, Paloma traces its lineage to avant-garde constructivist alphabets by Herbert Bayer, Jan Tschichold and Max Bill, translating their reductive formal logic into a full working type system. Seven weights run from Thin to Ultra with a corresponding variable font, all underpinned by 33,753 kerning pairs courtesy of spacing specialist Igino Marini. Coverage extends to 97 Latin-based languages alongside basic Greek, making it practically broader than its experimental origins might suggest.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Paloma as translating the most basic of shapes — triangle, circle, square — into legible letterforms, forming a visually unique and systematically consistent archetype family. It draws from avant-garde constructivist alphabets by Herbert Bayer, Jan Tschichold and Max Bill, and was first drawn for the Bikini Books visual identity before being extended into a multi-weight design covering 97 Latin-based languages with 33,753 kerning pairs.

name: Pareto
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Erkin Karamemet
classification_notes: A chunky slab serif with three interchangeable serif termination styles (rectangular, triangular, circular), drawing from the lineage of American display slabs while filtered through a Bauhaus sensibility.
summary: A display slab in three flavours, each defined by a different serif termination shape, rectangular, triangular or circular, sold separately or as a bundle.
description: Designed by Dinamo (Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb and Erkin Karamemet), Pareto draws on American frontier display type but routes it through a Bauhaus geometric logic, producing three structurally related fonts that share the same skeleton while differing only in serif shape. The three variants can be purchased individually or together, allowing the shapes to be mixed across a layout for a modular, comedic effect. Character coverage spans Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Pareto as informed by the long lineage of slab serifs that once appeared on the swing doors of American frontier saloons, but note it is more Bauhaus Western than Spaghetti Western. Three serif endings, circular, triangular and rectangular, can be mixed and matched to create a chunky, flexible and humorous modular system, available as individual files or bundled together.

name: Pelikan
designer: Fabian Harb, Fabiola Mejía
classification_notes: A blunt, unpolished sans-serif with an unusually large x-height and raw geometric qualities that place it firmly in the grotesque tradition, distinguished by its built-in unicase feature.
summary: A raw, blunt grotesque from Dinamo with a built-in unicase feature that sets uppercase and lowercase letters to the same height, reviving a short-lived 1970s typographic experiment.
description: Designed by Fabian Harb and Fabiola Mejía at Dinamo, Pelikan carries an unusually large x-height and an unpolished character that sets it apart from refined grotesques. Its two unicase stylistic sets offer distinct modes: one solid and text-friendly, the other playfully jumbled. The family spans five widths including Condensed, Extended, Mono and Semi Mono, each with eight weights and matching italics, with Latin, Greek and Cyrillic coverage.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Pelikan as an unpolished sans with a unicase feature baked in, letting you extend all letters to the same height. They trace the concept to Modernist designers of the 1920s who campaigned against capital letters and to 1970s designers who pursued an egalitarian type treatment. Pelikan brings this idea into the 21st century with a raw, blunt yet approachable feel and a confident stance built on its large x-height.

name: Prophet
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Erkin Karamemet
classification_notes: Prophet bridges calligraphic warmth drawn from Joseph Churchward's Georgina with a clean, mechanical structure, placing it firmly in the humanist sans category with a distinctive hand-influenced quality.
summary: A humanist sans by Dinamo co-founders Johannes Breyer and Fabian Harb, drawing on Joseph Churchward's Georgina to marry calligraphic curves with a clean, machine-like structure.
description: Released in 2016, Prophet was the first typeface Breyer and Harb worked on together, making it something of a founding document for Dinamo as a studio. Six weights from Thin to Extrabold each carry a corresponding italic, with a variable font also available. The character set spans Latin Extended and Greek, and features include alternate lowercase 'a' and tabular figures. Seen in use across editorial, art publishing and independent fashion contexts.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Prophet as inspired by Joseph Churchward's Georgina, combining warm calligraphic curves with a clean, machine-like sensibility that bridges the hand-drawn and mechanical genres. It holds the distinction of being the first typeface the two Dinamo co-founders worked on together, and was developed collaboratively across three countries and two continents before its 2016 release.

name: Publisher Rounded
designer: Michelangelo Nigra, Fabian Harb, Marc Rouault
classification_notes: Publisher draws directly from the typewriter genre, offering a Slab subfamily alongside a Sans and a hybrid Mix, all sharing the same skeleton. The Slab roots and typewriter heritage place it squarely in the slab category, though the family's breadth spans styles that blur into sans-serif territory.
summary: A proportionally spaced typewriter-inspired family from Dinamo, spanning Slab, Sans and a hybrid Mix across six weights with condensed variants and full variable font support.
description: Rooted in the typewriter genre but freed from its monospaced constraints, Publisher offers three distinct subfamilies: a classic Slab, a clean Sans and a playful Mix that blends both. All three share the same underlying skeleton, making it straightforward to combine them across a single project without visual inconsistency. Six weights with italics, condensed cuts for each subfamily and variable font packaging give the family a range of application rarely seen in typewriter-derived designs.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Publisher as a typewriter-inspired design re-upholstered for modern print and screen use. They trace the genre from early monospaced designs like Courier through proportional interpretations like ITC American Typewriter, positioning Publisher's three subfamilies — Slab, Sans and Mix — as sharing the same design DNA so they can be mixed freely. Variable fonts cover the full weight and width range for fine-grained control.

name: ROM
designer: Seb McLauchlan
classification_notes: ROM combines the rational structure of classic Grotesque with raw details drawn from Gothic typefaces, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition with an additional Gothic influence that gives it a distinctive character.
summary: A hybrid grotesque and gothic sans by Seb McLauchlan, taking proportional cues from conceptual art catalogs of the 1960s and 70s and spanning six width subfamilies plus a mono.
description: Designed by graphic designer and type-maker Seb McLauchlan, ROM fuses the rationalised construction of classic Grotesque with raw Gothic details, producing a typeface that shifts between the elegant and the dissonant. The family spans six subfamilies from Compressed to Wide, including a monospaced variant, each offered in seven weights from Light to Black with matching italics, all unified under a single variable font. Caps are wide and generous; lowercase proportions are narrow and refined, a contrast drawn from the typographic language of '60s and '70s conceptual art publishing.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ROM as a sturdy, confident fusion of classic Grotesk and Gothic styles designed by their longstanding collaborator Seb McLauchlan, with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They note that McLauchlan, a graphic designer first, set out to create a mature typeface in the spirit of Franklin Gothic, Bell Gothic and Akzidenz-Grotesk for his own publishing projects, requiring a complete weight range he could use sparingly. The six subfamilies flow from Compressed to Extended, and Dinamo highlight how the O's counter shifts from diamond to rectangle to circle across the width range as evidence of ROM's varied influences brought together under one variable roof.

name: Repro
designer: Erkin Karamemet
classification_notes: Repro has an overall geometric feel but with softer, more personable qualities; it is explicitly described as a friendly sans inspired by signage and digital operating systems, placing it within the geometric sans tradition.
summary: A geometric sans by Erkin Karamemet that pairs nine weights and italics with a monospaced variant, a variable font axis and a built-in circled-glyph system for creating visual hierarchy.
description: Designed by Erkin Karamemet and spaced by Igino Marini, Repro takes cues from signage and digital operating systems while softening the mechanical rigidity typical of geometric sans families. The family ships with nine weights from Hairline to Ultra, matching italics, a full monospaced subfamily and a variable font, making it equally suited to screen interfaces and print. An unusual set of OpenType features includes a circled-emphasis tool, sharp and soft alternates for individual characters, and web-specific glyphs, giving designers considerable control over the typeface's rhythm and character.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Repro as a friendly, flexible sans serif inspired by signage and digital operating systems, merging clean design with complex font engineering. The extensive family covers nine weights with italics plus a monospaced variant and numerous OpenType features, forming a toolkit for online and offline experiences. As a customisable variable font it is built with large system functionality and digital interfaces in mind, and includes a unique circled-emphasis tool alongside a range of sharp or soft alternates.

name: Schengen
designer: Seb McLauchlan with Luke Charsley
classification_notes: A geometric sans system drawing on Helvetica and Eurostile as reference points, with proportional and monospaced variants across multiple widths, plus expressive offshoots with slab and inline treatments.
summary: A sprawling nine-family type system built over six years, drawing on Eurozone logistics and infrastructure as its visual and thematic foundation.
description: Designed by Seb McLauchlan with Luke Charsley, the system organises itself around three proportional families, A, B and C, which shift aesthetic register as their width increases, moving from Helvetica-like softness toward the boxy authority of Eurostile. Each has a monospaced equivalent, and three further families, Core, Line and Zone, extend the system into slab, inline and stripped-back territory. Latin, Cyrillic and Greek coverage means the family can handle all European languages implicated in the cross-border transport networks that inspired it.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Schengen as a multi-genre collection inspired by the Eurozone's visual language of cross-border logistics, manufacturing and construction. The A, B and C families share a width axis that lets users slide between Helvetica-soft and Eurostile-boxy extremes, while Core, Line and Zone push the system into expressive infrastructure territory. All nine families share the same structural dimensions for effortless mixing.

name: Social
designer: Johannes Breyer, Fabian Harb, Wei Huang, Malte Bentzen, Erkin Karamemet, Fabiola Mejía
classification_notes: A friendly, smooth grotesque with rounded forms, double-storey 'a' and a dynamic 's', distinguished from more constructed geometric sans serifs in the Dinamo library.
summary: A multi-width, variable grotesque from a large collaborative team at Dinamo, covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek with rounded, freely drawn forms and a resident sense of humour.
description: Designed by a team of six at Dinamo with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, Social spans four width variants including a monospaced cut, eight weights from Hairline to Black, and matching italics throughout. Its forms are notably warm and unforced for a grotesque, with a dynamic 's', double-storey 'a' and three distinct punctuation styles: square, round and angled. Variable Font support covers slant, width and weight axes, making it unusually flexible across screen and print contexts.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Social as a multi-people, multi-width mega collaboration made by many eyes and many players. They position it as a friendly, smooth grotesque that fills a gap in their library, standing apart from their more constructed sans serifs both visually and technically. The name references how the project came about and how designing it felt, with the hope that still more people might meet and connect through working with the font.

name: Solar
designer: Renan Rosatti
classification_notes: Solar is explicitly rooted in the geometric sans tradition, citing Kabel, Futura, Metro No. 2, Johnston and Erbar-Grotesque as reference points, with a nearly circular capital O and single-storey construction throughout.
summary: A highly customisable geometric sans from Dinamo, drawing on early 20th-century references from Kabel to Futura, with eight stylistic sets and a dedicated display subfamily.
description: Designed by Renan Rosatti at Dinamo, Solar offers eight weights with matching italics across two optical subfamilies: Solar for text and Solar Display for tight, punchy headlines. Its defining characteristic is a near-circular capital O, balanced against open counters and straight terminals in characters like c and s that introduce a sense of fluidity within the geometry. Eight stylistic sets reference typefaces including Johnston, Metro No. 2 and Erbar-Grotesque, allowing the typeface's character to shift considerably through OpenType alternates.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Solar as a highly customisable geometric sans with a large weight spectrum and matching italics, sitting in the space between remix and homage of early 20th-century geometric and humanistic typefaces. Its round shapes, simple single-storey constructions and open-counter characters give it fluidity within its geometry, while eight stylistic sets let users shift its personality across a wide range.

name: Synt
designer: Kaj Lehmann
classification_notes: Synt is described as a reimagining of 19th century modern faces, drawing from a typeface used in an 1900 book by Theodore Low De Vinne, placing it firmly in the didone/modern serif tradition with its emphatic vertical contrast and unitised rhythm.
summary: A unitised reimagining of 19th century modern faces by Kaj Lehmann, built on a parametric stem rhythm and three distinct styles of italic slant.
description: Drawn from a typeface used in Theodore Low De Vinne's 1900 printing, Synt brings a strict parametric rhythm to the didone tradition, maintaining equal spacing between and within every stem. The family spans six weights with true italics at 16°, a slanted variant at the same angle and a Turbo Slant at 32°, all accessible via a variable font file. A monospaced cut and a set of musical glyphs extend its usefulness across apps, podcasting and streaming platforms.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Synt as a unitised reimagining of 19th century modern faces, taking its logic from a typeface used in an influential 1900 book by commercial printer Theodore Low De Vinne and remixing it with special glyphs and musical alternates suited to apps, podcasting and streaming. Its parametric rhythm places equal distance between and within every stem, while the full family offers six weights, a mono cut and three italic styles including a Turbo Slant at 32°.

name: Walter
designer: Renan Rosatti, Arnaud Chemin, Simon Mager, Leonardo Azzolini
classification_notes: Walter spans faithful revivals of Walter Käch's mid-century letterform teaching tools (Walter Alte) and a contemporary grotesque extension (Walter Neue), both rooted in Swiss grotesque tradition with varying degrees of contrast and construction.
summary: A joint scholarly project by Dinamo and Omnigroup reviving the letterform teaching tools of Swiss type educator Walter Käch, extended into a fully contemporary grotesque family.
description: Walter Alte offers three faithfully revived cuts drawn directly from Käch's mid-century pedagogical drawings, ranging from the high-contrast Röntgentherapie to the constructed monolinear Normalgrotesk. Walter Neue departs from strict revival to offer fourteen styles across seven weights with italics and stylistic alternates, softening the source material with rounded punctuation and friendlier proportions suited to text and display use. Walter Neue Mono mirrors the Neue family in a monospaced variant, completing a three-branch system that runs from historical study to functional contemporary type.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe ABC Walter as a long-standing joint scholarly effort with Omnigroup, celebrating type educator Walter Käch, whose teaching tools left fingerprints on Switzerland's most famous fonts. Rather than reviving an existing typeface, the project departs from Käch's letterform drawing instruction system: Walter Alte faithfully studies his lessons across three distinct cuts, while Walter Neue extends and interprets those teachings as a typeface in its own right, softer and less rigid, with rounded dots and punctuation that lend it a friendlier character.

name: Whyte
designer: Fabian Harb, Johannes Breyer, Erkin Karamemet, Fabiola Mejía
classification_notes: Dinamo describe Whyte as a contemporary grotesque with idiosyncrasies; the ink trap variant and smooth/sharp transitions are hallmarks of post-digital grotesque revival design.
summary: A contemporary grotesque from Dinamo spanning six subfamilies, ten weights and five scripts, with ink trap variants that can be tuned via variable font axes.
description: Designed by Fabian Harb and Johannes Breyer at Dinamo with Erkin Karamemet and Fabiola Mejía, Whyte pairs smooth and sharp stroke transitions in its standard cut, while the Inktrap subfamily repurposes a hot-metal-era printing technique as a visible design feature, deepened or shortened via a dedicated variable axis. The family stretches to Mono and Semi Mono variants, each available with or without ink traps, and all six subfamilies run ten weights from Thin to Super with matching italics. Script coverage extends to Greek, Cyrillic, Hangul and Hebrew, making it one of the more globally prepared grotesques available from a single source.
foundry_blurb: Dinamo describe Whyte as a contemporary grotesque filled with idiosyncrasies and character, available in two distinctive subfamilies: the standard cut with smooth and sharp transitions, and the Inktrap cut, which uses variable font technology to let users deepen or shorten ink traps for the first time, turning what was once a functional printing necessity into a sculptural design detail.
